entries.ocr_text:
PREFACE .
The original Code of Civil Practice was adopted by the Legislature in
March, 1851 ; this Code was revised and amended , a Criminal Code pre-
pared, and, in 1854 , the Codes of Practice in civil and criminal cases were
adopted.
The present Codes were prepared , under an act of the Legislature , by
Richard A. Buckner and Joshua F. Bullitt , Commissioners , and Alvin
Duvall, umpire ; were adopted by the Legislature in 1876 , and took effect
Jan 1, 1877 .
ORM edition contains all amendments to date, and a number of acts
rela directly to the subject - matter of the Codes . I have added at the
end of the Civil Code an appendix giving original and appellate jurisdic-
tion of courts in civil cases ; and article 2, chapter 66 , General Statutes
relating to rent-mode and manner of recovery .
The notes of decisions of the Court of Appeals have , as a rule , been
confined to cases decided since 1851-but a number of notes of decisions
prior to that date have been inserted—and include cases reported in 84 Ky .
bringing notes down to January term, 1887 .
The sections in brackets refer to corresponding sections in the Codes of
1854 ; following the index to the Criminal Code is a table showing corres-
ponding sections of that Code and Code of 1854 .
I trust that the notes , references and forms will be found complete , the
arrangement convenient , and that , as a whole , the book will meet with
favor and prove useful to the profession .
I desire to acknowledge my deep obligations to Judge Joseph Barbour
for valuable suggestions and advice frequently asked for , and cheerfully
given . To E. W. Hines , Esq . , I am indebted for his courtesy in furnishing
me advance pages of 84 Ky .
NEW CASTLE, KY. , September , 1888 .
JOHN D. CARROLL .
2771-1
dan

entries.ocr_text:
# AN ACT

REGULATING

## PRACTICE IN CIVIL CASES.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, as follows:*

### TITLE I.
#### PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS.

*   § 1. Civil cases defined.
*   § 2. Civil actions defined.
*   § 3. Special proceedings.

**§ 1. Civil cases defined.** Civil cases are actions or special proceedings.

**§ 2. Civil actions defined.** A civil action is a demand, by pleadings, in a court of justice, for the enforcement of an alleged right of a plaintiff against a defendant. (*"Action" further defined, sec. 732-34.*)

**§ 3. Special proceedings.** Every other civil case is a special proceeding.

---

### TITLE II.
#### FORM OF CIVIL ACTIONS.

*   § 4. Form of action.
*   § 5. Kinds of actions.
*   § 6. When action must be equitable; when ordinary.
*   § 7. Action may be brought on lost or mutilated writing.
*   § 8. Error as to form of action may be corrected.
*   § 9. How error corrected.
*   § 10. How and when action may be transferred from one docket to the other.
*   § 11. How ordinary action may be transferred to equity.

---

**§ 1. Provisional remedy not cause of action.** A provisional remedy is not a cause of action; it is merely an incident to the action in which it is sought. *Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met. 97.*

**§ 3. (1.) Bond—motion for judgment on.** Motion for judgment on a bond executed pursuant to sec. 645 is a special proceeding. *Watson v. Gabby, 18 B. M. 658.* In such proceedings under Code of 1854, written pleadings were not allowed, but sec. 449 of present Code permits written pleadings. *Couchman v. Maupin, 78 Ky. 33.*

# AN ACT
REGULATING
## PRACTICE IN CIVIL CASES.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, as follows:*

### TITLE I.
#### PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS.

*   § 1. Civil cases defined.
*   § 2. Civil actions defined.
*   § 3. Special proceedings.

**§ 1. Civil cases defined.** Civil cases are actions or special proceedings.

**§ 2. Civil actions defined.** A civil action is a demand, by pleadings, in a court of justice, for the enforcement of an alleged right of a plaintiff against a defendant. (*"Action" further defined, sec. 732-34.*)

**§ 3. Special proceedings.** Every other civil case is a special proceeding.

---

### TITLE II.
#### FORM OF CIVIL ACTIONS.

*   § 4. Form of action.
*   § 5. Kinds of actions.
*   § 6. When action must be equitable; when ordinary.
*   § 7. Action may be brought on lost or mutilated writing.
*   § 8. Error as to form of action may be corrected.
*   § 9. How error corrected.
*   § 10. How and when action may be transferred from one docket to the other.
*   § 11. How ordinary action may be transferred to equity.

---

**§ 1. Provisional remedy not cause of action.** A provisional remedy is not a cause of action; it is merely an incident to the action in which it is sought. *Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met. 97.*

**§ 3. (1.) Bond—motion for judgment on.** Motion for judgment on a bond executed pursuant to sec. 645 is a special proceeding. *Watson v. Gabby, 18 B. M. 658.* In such proceedings under Code of 1854, written pleadings were not allowed, but sec. 449 of present Code permits written pleadings. *Couchman v. Maupin, 78 Ky. 33.*

**(2.) Bastardy case.** Bastardy case is special proceeding. *Chandler v. Com., 4 Met. 67.* *Francis v. Com., 3 Bush 4.*

2

classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the forms of civil actions, distinguishing between 'ordinary' and 'equitable' actions. It details when each type of action should be brought and clarifies that while forms of action have been abolished, the substance of common law rules of legal procedure remains. It provides examples of actions that fall under equitable jurisdiction (e.g., bills quia timet, actions on return of 'no property', surety actions, sale of property of infants, relief for partial failure of consideration, fraud, mistake, trust estates, divorces, injunctions, partition, dower, liens) and those that should be brought as ordinary actions (e.g., assault and battery, assumpsit, covenant, debt, recovery of possession of real estate, fraud or deceit in sale of property, malicious prosecution, seduction, slander, libel, trespass, recovery of rent).

It focuses on the classification and handling of legal cases based on their nature and historical jurisdiction. Form of Civil Actions .

according to the provisions of section 6, it should have been an
equitable action .
3. The court may, without motion , order the transfer of an
action from one docket to another if either party be entitled to
such transfer ; or may try the action , or render judgment therein
by default ,unless a party entitled to a transfer move therefor .
§ 11 [ 10 ] . How ordinary action may be transferred to equity .
In an ordinary action properly commenced as such-
1. If there be several issues all of which were, before the first
day of August , 1851 , cognizable in chancery, though none were
exclusively so , either party may , by motion , have the case trans-
ferred to the equity docket .
2. Either party may, by motion , have the case transferred to
the equity docket for the trial of any issue which , before the said
day , was exclusively cognizable in chancery .
3. If there be an issue which was not cognizable in chancery ,
and an issue which was exclusively cognizable in chancery ,
before the said day, the plaintiff may have the former issue
tried before the latter is disposed of.
4. If there be an issue which was not cognizable in chancery ,
and an issue which was cognizable in chancery, but not exclu-
sively so , before the said day, the case shall not be transferred
to the equity docket without consent of the parties .
§ 11. ( 1. ) When defendant not entitled to transfer . In an ordinary action ,
properly commenced as such, the defendant is not entitled to a transfer of the case
unless the answer presents a valid equitable defense . Jones v. Letcher , 13 B. M. 363 ,
(2. ) Equitable defense. When an answer filed in an ordinary action contains
a valid equitable defense , no judgment can be rendered until some disposition is
made of the answer , and in such case the action should be transferred to equity .
Bosley v. Mattingly , 14 B. M. 89 ; Harreld v. Howard , 80 Ky . 51 .
( 3. ) Equitable and legal defense . If the answer to an ordinary action presents
an equitable and a legal defense , the equitable issue should be tried by the court and
the legal issue referred to a jury , unless a jury is waived . Both issues should be dis-
posed of before judgment . Smith v. Moberly, 15 B. M. 70 ; Petty v. Malier, 15 B. М.
591; Geoghegan v. Ditto, 2 Met. 433 ; Bennett v. Titherington , 6 Bush 192 .
(4.) Where legal and equitable issues are formed in an ordinary action , the court
should transfer only the equitable issue to equity , because if the equitable defense tail ,
the defendant is entitled to a jury to try the legal issue ; but if the equitable issue is
a complete defense to the action , the refusal to submit legal issue to a jury is not error.
Wimmer v. Ficklin , 14 Bush 193 .
(5. ) Error in transferring ordinary action . If the plaintiff in an ordinary
action is entitled to a jury trial, it is error to transfer action to equity , and he is not
concluded by the judgment dismissing his petition , although he failed to take proof
to support his cause of action . Creager v . Walker , 7 Bush 1.

until all issues not transferred are tried or disposed of in favor of
the plaintiff . ( Form of affidavit , page 405. Form of bond , page 403.)
§ 15 [12] . Waiver of error as to form of action . An error as
to the kind of proceedings adopted in the action is waived by failure
to move for its correction at the time and in the manner prescribed in
this chapter ; and errors in the decisions of the court , on any of the
motions named in this chapter, are waived , unless excepted to at the
time-which may be done by the clerk noting , at the end of such
decision , words of the following import : " To which decision the
plaintiff (or defendant) excepts ." ( See further as to exceptions ,
sec . 333. )
§ 16 [13 ] . Provisions of Code apply to all civil actions . The
provisions of this Code apply to all civil actions, unless the contrary
appears .
§ 17 [14] . When ordinary judgment corrected in equity. A
judgment obtained in an ordinary action shall not be annulled nor
§ 17. ( 1. ) Common law judgment -correction of by chancellor . The chan-
cellor has no jurisdiction to cancel , set aside or correct common law judgment , except
for some cause of new trial under other provisions of the Code , or for defense arising
or discovered after judgment . Emmerson v. Herriford , 8 Bush 229 ; Farmers ' Bank
v. Collins , 13 Bush 138 ; Com . v. Cain , 80 Ky . 318 .
(2.) Usury. If defendant permits judgment for usury to go against him without
interposing a defense known to him at the time , he can not afterwards have the judg-
ment modified . Chinn v. Mitchell , 2 Met . 92 ; Thomasson v. Townsend , 10 Bush 114 .
(3. ) If plaintiff, after obtaining judgment for usury , become a non -resident , the
defendant may , in a suit to recover the usury , have it set off against the judgment .
Moss v. Rowland, 1 Duv. 321 .
(4.) Usury paid upon a judgment at law can not be recovered back at law . Pierce
v. Hedrick , 3 Litt. 109 ; Thompson v. Ware , 8 B. M. 28. Nor can usury paid upon a
decree in equity be recovered back in equity . Moran v. Woodyard , 8 B. M. 538. But
usury embraced in common law judgment may be recovered back in equity . Ross
v. Ross , 3 Met. 273 .
(5.) Attorney fee. When a judgment is rendered by default for an attorney fee
stipulated in the writing sued on , and properly set up , the defendant will be without
remedy . Thomasson v. Townsend , 10 Bush 114.
(6. ) Defense known . If the matter of defense is substantially within the knowl-
edge of defendant at the time judgment is rendered against him, he will not be
permitted to annul or modify the judgment . McCown v. Macklin , 7 Bush 308. See
notes to sec . 518 as to vacation of judgments .

CHAP. I.] PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 25

Parties to Actions Generally.

and if the assignment be not authorized by statute the assignor must be a party, as plaintiff or defendant. This section does not apply to bills of exchange, nor to promissory notes placed upon the footing of bills of exchange, nor to common orders or checks. (*When assignee required to secure costs, sec. 619.*)

§ 20 [32]. **In whose name prosecuted if claim assigned after action commenced.** If the right of the plaintiff be transferred or

(22.) Action against officers of a corporation for misconduct should be brought by the corporation; but if it be in the hands of such officers, the stockholders may sue in equity. *Jones v. Johnson*, 10 Bush 649, 79 Ky. 300.
(23.) Action lies in corporate name to recover stock subscribed to the corporation before its organization, and made payable to the president and directors. *Lackey v. R & L. T. P. Co.*, 17 B. M. 43.
(24.) **Officer's bond.** Action on may be brought by a party in interest in his own name, if there is any covenant in the bond to the benefit of which he is entitled. *Hughes v. Cotton*, 13 Bush 596; *Lane v. Kasey*, 1 Met. 410.
(25.) Action on may be brought in name of Commonwealth for benefit of party injured. *Com. v. Teal*, 14 B. M. 29.
(26.) **Members of church.** Members of a church who have been ex-communicated can not maintain an action concerning the church property. *Shannon v. Frost*, 3 B. M. 253.
(27.) **Bank check.** Holder of a check who can trace legal title to it may maintain action on it, whether he possesses beneficial interest in it or not. *Harpending v. Daniel*, 80 Ky. 449.
(28.) The holder of an unaccepted check on bankers may maintain action on it against them, if drawer had sufficient deposit to pay it at the time it was drawn, and they were notified it was drawn on them. *Lester v. Given*, 8 Bush 357.

§ 19. As to what promissory notes are placed on the footing of bills of exchange, see Gen. Stat., chap. 22, sec. 21.

(1.) **Bills of exchange.** Partial or total failure of consideration, or fraud, is no defense against a *bona fide* holder of a bill for a valuable consideration before it becomes due. *Kelly v. Smith*, 1 Met. 313; *Spencer v. Biggs*, 2 Met. 123.
(2.) A bill of exchange or promissory note taken after it becomes due subjects the holder to all the equities attaching to it in the hands of the person from whom he received it. *Lester v. Given*, 8 Bush 357; *Greenwell v. Haydon*, 78 Ky. 332.
(3.) The holder of commercial paper who has received it as collateral security for an antecedent debt is not a *bona fide* holder. 78 Ky. 332, 1 Met. 628.
(4.) A note in the hands of one who has procured it to be discounted in a bank, and afterward taken it up, is subject to any defense that might have been made against it before the discount. *Cline v. Templeton*, 78 Ky. 550.

§ 20. (1.) **Assignment pending suit—costs.** Where a right of action is assigned pending the suit, and the assignee substituted on the record without objection from the adverse party, the original party is released from liability for costs. *Warner v. Turner*, 18 B. M. 758.
(2.) This section vests a discretion in the court to be exercised in the furtherance of justice; and when the party to whom assignment is made is substituted security should be required for past and future costs. *Dougherty v. Smith*, 4 Met. 278.
(3.) **Compromise after notice of transfer.** After a suit has been transferred to a third person not a party to the action, and the defendant notified of the transfer, he can not compromise with the plaintiff to the prejudice of the assignee. *Cantrell v. Hewlett*, 2 Bush 311.

26 [TITLE III .PARTIES OF CIVIL ACTIONS .
Parties to Actious Generally .
assigned during the pendency of the action, it may be continued in
his name ; or the court may allow the person to whom the transfer or
assignment is made to be substituted in the action, proper orders
being made as to security for the costs .
§ 21 [ 33 ] . Action by fiduciary or person authorized by
statute . A personal representative, guardian , curator , committee of
a person of unsound mind , trustee of an express trust , a person with
whom or in whose name a contract is made for the benefit of another ,
a receiver appointed by a court , the assignee of a bankrupt , or a person
expressly authorized by statute to do so , may bring an action without
joining with him the person for whose benefit it is prosecuted . ( Who
is " Personal representative ," Sec . 732-17 .)
§ 21. ( 1.) Trustee-when may sue alone . Trustee can sue at law for property
to which he holds the legal title , and in equity where he is entitled to receive and hold
the property or money for the benefit of the cestui que trust without making the latter
party. But trustee under a mortgage made to secure the payment of money to others
can not sue for a foreclosure and sale without making them parties . B. & L. R. R.
Co. v. Metcalfe , 4 Met. 199. See note 3.
(2. ) Trustee to whom property is conveyed for the separate use of a married
woman may sue for it in his own name . McClanahan v. Beasley , 17 В. М. 111 .
(3. ) Trustee holding inortgage on land for certain bondholders is trustee of an
express trust, and can maintain suit for sale of land without joining with him persons
for whose benefit the suit is prosecuted . Krieger v. Bissell , 80 Ky . 330. The court
makes a distinction between this case and case in 4 Met. 199. See note 1.
(4.) Where a will conferred upon a trustee under it power to name his successor ,
the cestui que trust was not a necessary party to an action by the trustee to have a
successor appointed . Barclay v. Goodloe , 83 Ky . 493 .
( 5. ) Executor . Executor who by will is directed to lease a ferry can maintain in
his own name an action in equity to be quieted in the enjoyment of the franchise .
City of Newport v. Taylor, 16 B. M. 699 .
( 6. ) Administrator refusing to sue-heir may . Personal representative refusing
to sue, heir or distributee may sue to recover property due the intestate , but the per-
sonal representative is a necessary party . McChord v. Fisher, 13 B. M. 193 .
( 7. ) Guardian to sue in name of ward . Guardian can not sue in his own
name for personal property of his ward unlawfully detained. The suit must be in
name of infant by the guardian . Anderson v. Watson , 3 Met . 509 .
(8.) Guardian can not maintain in his own name action to eject tenant of his ward
after expiration of lease ; suit must be in name of ward . Powell v. Gossom , 18 B. М.
179 , 13 В. М. 193 .
(9. ) Guardian can sue in his own name upon a note taken by him for money of
his ward . McChord v. Fisher, 13 B. M. 193 ; 3 Met . 509 .
( 10. ) Sheriff to whom estate committed . Sheriff to whom an estate is com-
mitted as administrator de bonis non may sue upon notes executed to the former
representative , and which came into his hands as assets . Maraman v. Trunnel , 3
Met . 145 .
(11. ) Receiver . Receiver can not bring an action involving title to realty without
joining with him the parties in interest . Caldwell v. McWhorter , 84 Ky . 130.
(12. ) Assignee . Assignee should bring suit to set aside fraudulent conveyance
made by a bankrupt . Anderson v. Anderson , 80 Ky . 638 .

28 [TITLE III .PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS .
Parties to Actions Generally .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Parties to Civil Actions
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines rules for parties involved in civil lawsuits. It specifies when one or more individuals can sue or defend on behalf of a larger group with a common interest or when the parties are too numerous to be brought before the court individually. It also details various scenarios for necessary or proper parties in different types of legal actions, such as assignees, beneficiaries, infants, administrators, sureties, counties, married women, and public officials.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890, specifically dealing with rules of civil procedure regarding parties to actions. It outlines general legal principles for who can be involved in a lawsuit. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

CHAP. 1.] PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 29

Parties to Actions Generally.

to bills of exchange, to promissory notes placed upon the footing of bills of exchange, or to common orders and checks, and sureties on the same or separate instruments, may all or any of them, or the representatives of such as may have died, be included in the same action at the plaintiff's option. (*Judgment against secs. 370, 373; when can not be rendered, sec. 80.*)

§ 27 [39]. **Action against persons jointly bound on contract.** If two or more persons be jointly bound by contract, the action thereon may be brought against all or any of them, at the plaintiff's option. If any of the persons so bound be dead, the action may be brought against any or all of the survivors with the representatives of all or any of the decedents, or against the latter or any of them. If all the persons so bound be dead, the action may be brought against the representatives of all or of any of them. An action or judgment against any one or more of several persons jointly bound shall not be a bar to proceedings against the others. (*Judgment against, secs. 370, 373; when can not be rendered, sec. 80.*)

§ 28 [40, 400]. **Court may require new parties to be made.** The court may determine any controversy between parties before it,

(3.) **Guarantors.** Several signers of contract of guaranty for the payment each of $100 may be sued jointly. Wilde v. Haycraft, 2 Duv. 309.
(1.) **Sub-lessee and assignee.** A lessee may sue jointly a sub-lessee and his assignee upon a contract of the sub-lessee to pay him rent. Trabue v. McAdams, 8 Bush 74.

§ 27. (1.) **Separate action against joint obligors.** The common law rule has been changed by the Code, and persons jointly liable may be sued separately. Waits v. McClure, 10 Bush 763; Gossom v. Badgett, 6 Bush 97; 1 Duv. 30.
(2.) **Partners may be sued severally.** One or all of the partners may be sued by a creditor of the firm, and judgment against one or more will not be a bar to proceeding against the others. Williams v. Rogers, 14 Bush 776; Moore v. Estes, 79 Ky. 282, overruling Nichols v. Burton, 5 Bush 320, in so far as it holds that judgment against one partner amounts to a satisfaction of the claim against other partners. See further notes to secs. 370,373.

§ 28. (1.) **Court may require new parties made.** When record shows that money sought to be recovered from a garnishee belongs to one not a party, court should require owner brought before it. Forepaugh v. Appold, 17 B. M. 625.
(2.) In an action by a stockholder, where the corporation was an indispensable party, the court say: "It was not proper for the court to require the plaintiff to bring the corporation before it. This was plaintiff's duty, as in the absence of the corporation no relief could be granted." Shawhan v. Zinn, 79 Ky. 300.
(3.) **Court—duty of concerning parties.** The court should order all persons interested in the controversy to be brought before it, although they have agreed not to sue until the happening of a certain event. Lou. v. Henderson, 5 Bush 515. See Com. v. Frankfort, 13 Bush 185. And if, upon hearing the evidence, the court should believe that a party mentioned in the pleadings has such an interest as to render him a necessary party, he should be brought in. Vanbuskirk v. Levy, 3 Met. 133.

CHAP. II. PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 31
Parties to Certain Actions.

to, or interest in, the property or its proceeds, may, before payment of the proceeds to the plaintiff, file, in the action, his verified petition, stating his claim and controverting that of the plaintiff; whereupon the court may order him to be made a defendant; and upon that being done, his petition shall be treated as his answer. But if he be a non-resident he must give security for costs. (*Claimant of attached property to give information, sec. 220; see also, sec. 30.*)

CHAPTER II.
*Parties to Certain Actions.*

*   § 30. New party may be made upon affidavit of defendant.
*   § 31. Provisions of section 30 apply to officer who is sued.
*   § 32. Plaintiff in writ may be substituted as defendant in action against officer.
*   § 33. Action by tenant to recover property distrained.

§ 30 [42]. **New party may be made upon affidavit of defendant.** Upon affidavit of a defendant before answer, in an action upon contract, or for the recovery of personal property, that a person who is not a party to the action, without collusion with him, makes a claim to the subject of the action, and that the affiant is ready to pay or dispose thereof as the court may direct, the court may make an order for the safe-keeping of the subject of the action, or for its payment or deposit in court, or for its delivery to such person as the court may direct, and an order requiring such alleged claimant to appear in a reasonable time and maintain or relinquish his claim against the defendant, and, in the meantime, stay the proceeding. If such alleged claimant, being served with a copy of the order, fail to appear, the court may declare him barred of all claim in respect to the subject of the action against the defendant therein. If he appear he shall be allowed to make himself defendant in the action, in lieu of the original defendant, who shall be discharged from all liability to either of the other parties in respect to the subject of the action

§ 30. (1.) **Affidavit required—object of.** The affidavit required by this section is designed to take the place of the more formal pleading required before the Code, and it must appear that there is no collusion between the parties. Starling v. Brown, 7 Bush 164.
(2.) **Money wrongfully obtained—disposition of.** Money, although wrongfully obtained, being produced to the court, is a fund in court, and when claimed by more than one party the court should, where the pleadings justify it, submit the question of ownership to a jury, and order the money paid over according to the verdict. Davis v. Watkins, 2 Bush 224. See 3 J. J. M. 64; 5 J. J. M. 235; 7 Dana 411, for decisions upon questions of interpleader before the Code.

§ 33 [ 45 ] . Action by tenant to recover property distrained .
An action to recover the possession of specific personal property

have no other claim to hold it than that derived from the warrant .
The indorsement of a levy on the property , made upon the warrant

CHAP. III.] PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 33

Coverture.

ARTICLE I.

Coverture.

§ 34. Actions by and against married women.
1. When may sue and be sued alone.
2. Husband must be joined.
3. May defend for self and husband.
4. When deserted by husband may sue and defend.
5. When woman marries, husband to be made party.
6. Proceedings if of unsound mind or imprisoned.

§ 34 [49 to 52]. 1. **Actions by and against married women.**
In actions between husband and wife; in actions concerning her

§ 34. (1.) **Husband—action by for injury to wife.** Husband may sue for injury to person of wife. Eden v. L. & T. R. R. Co., 14 B. M. 204.

(2.) **Wife can not sue alone—exceptions.** The common law rule is in force except in the cases mentioned in this section, and wife can not sue alone in any case not embraced by it; in an action for the personal suffering of, or injury to, the wife, the husband must join. A next friend can not prosecute a suit in the name of the infant wife against the wishes of her infant husband. Anderson v. Anderson, 11 Bush 327; 13 Bush 471.

(3.) **Object of this section.** The only object of this section (49, same as sec. 34-1 of this Code), is to dispense with the necessity for a next friend where the action concerns the separate property of the wife, or is between herself and husband. It confers no new right. Matson v. Matson, 4 Met. 262.

(4.) **When husband must join.** In action by wife to recover property not her separate estate her husband must be joined. Petty v. Malier, 14 B. M. 246.

(5.) Wife can not maintain in her own name an action to recover money won from her husband at gaming. Moore v. Settle, 82 Ky. 187.

(6.) **Wife may sue alone.** Wife abandoned has legal right to prosecute suit in name of herself and husband; and, while it is more strictly conformable to the rules of pleading that the husband should be a party, adverse party can not complain when his name is stricken from record at his instance. Stith v. Patterson, 3 Bush 132.

(7.) Land, the general estate of infant wife, was exchanged for other land, the husband and wife conveying by deed of general warranty; after wife became of age she might maintain an action in her name to cancel contract; her husband being bound by the covenant of warranty, his interest is antagonistic to hers. Hardin v. Gerard, 10 Bush 259.

(8.) Wife can sue on bond of indemnity to recover damages for selling her separate estate under an execution to pay her husband's debts. Campbell v. Galbreath, 12 Bush 459. Nor is the husband a necessary party to such an action when he does not claim any interest in the property sold. Bayse v. Brown, 78 Ky. 553. And may sue in her own name for an equitable settlement. Wright v. Arnold, 14 B. M. 638; Moore v. Moore, 14 B. M. 259.

(9.) A married woman who came to this State before the adoption of the General Statutes is entitled to the benefits of chap. 52, art. 2, sec. 10 of statute. M. & L. R. R. Co. v. Herrick, 13 Bush 122.

(10.) Where husband is insolvent and a drunkard, and makes no provision for the support of his family, the wife may sue alone to enforce a trust created for her benefit. Berry v. Norris, 1 Duv. 302.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines rules for civil actions involving married women, infants, prisoners, and persons of unsound mind. It details when a wife can sue or be sued alone, when she must join her husband, and provisions for her defense if her husband fails to act. It also addresses situations where a husband deserts his wife, or when a female party to an action marries. For individuals under disability (unsound mind, imprisonment), it specifies the role of a committee, curator, next friend, or guardian ad litem. The annotations provide case law examples related to these provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code deals with legal procedures and the capacity of certain individuals (married women, infants, persons of unsound mind, prisoners) to sue or be sued. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is entirely on legal standing and representation for those under specific civil disabilities. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAP. III.] PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 35

Other Persons Under Disability.

§ 35 [53, 58]. **Actions by infants, prisoners and persons of unsound mind.** Excepting married women—

1. The action of a person who is under disability must be brought by his *guardian*, curator, or committee, if he have one residing in this State, unless it be against his guardian, curator, or committee; or unless said guardian, curator, or committee *refuse* to sue, in which case he must be made a defendant.
2. The action of an adult, who is imprisoned in or out of this State, and who has no curator residing herein, or his action against his curator, may be brought by him, or, with his consent, by his next friend.
3. The action of an infant, or of a person of unsound mind, who resides in this State, and who has no guardian, curator, or committee residing herein, or whose guardian, curator, or committee refuses to sue, or his action against his guardian, curator, or committee may be brought by his next friend.
4. The action of an infant, or of a person of unsound mind, who resides in a foreign country, and who has a guardian, curator, or committee residing therein, may be brought by such guardian, curator, or committee, or by his next friend.

§ 36 [55, 60, 63]. **Defense by infants, prisoners and persons of unsound mind.** Excepting married women, if a defendant, who

---

§ 35. (1.) **Infant—agreement with to be pleaded.** Agreement of infant plaintiff to dismiss action must be pleaded, and can not be made available by motion. Hopkins v. Virgin, 11 Bush 677.
(2.) **Poor infant—costs.** Poor infant and insolvent next friend will be permitted to prosecute an action without securing costs, as provided in sec. 619. Westerfield v. Wilson, 12 Bush 125, and see sec. 37-2.
(3.) **Action must be in name of infant by guardian.** Wilson v. Unselt, 12 Bush 215; Anderson v. Watson, 3 Met. 509.
(4.) **Next friend—action by.** Next friend of infant female can not prosecute an action in her name against wishes of her infant husband. Anderson v. Anderson, 11 Bush 327.
(5.) **Infant must sue by guardian.** Infant having statutory guardian can only appear in court by guardian. If he declines to act, infant may appear by next friend, making guardian defendant. Miller v. Cabell, 81 Ky. 178. And the guardian alone has the right to prosecute the action for his ward, and the court has no power to displace him, except for valid cause. Walker v. Smyser, 80 Ky. 620.
(6.) **Infant—rescission of contract by.** Infant seeking to avoid contract on ground of fraud can not avail himself of his infancy unless he pleads it. Bryant v. Pottinger, 6 Bush 473.

§ 36. (1.) **Infant arriving at age may answer.** Infant who has answered by his guardian *ad litem* coming of age before decree may answer for himself. Mitchell v. Berry, 1 Met. 602.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1890.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890, Section 36
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the procedures for making parties to civil actions who are under disability. It specifies how infants, persons of unsound mind, and adult prisoners must be represented in legal proceedings, either by a guardian, committee, curator, or guardian ad litem. It also details the requirements for rendering judgments against such defendants and the duties of guardians ad litem.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with legal procedures for individuals under disability (infants, persons of unsound mind, prisoners) in civil actions. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAP. III.] PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS. 37
---
General Provisions Concerning Persons Under Disability.

ARTICLE III.
*General Provisions concerning Persons under Disability.*
§ 37. Who may sue as next friend—liability for costs.
§ 38. Appointment of guardian *ad litem*—his duties and compensation.

§ 37 1 [53, 58]. **Who may sue as next friend—liability for costs.** No person shall sue as next friend unless he reside in this State and be free from disability, nor unless he file his own affidavit showing his right to sue as next friend according to the provisions of this chapter.

2 [54, 59]. A guardian, curator, committee, or next friend who brings or prosecutes an action for a person who is under disability is liable for the costs which accrue during his conduct of the action, unless he be allowed to sue in *forma pauperis*, by an order of the court, or by an order of the judge thereof during vacation. (*Meaning of* "disability," *sec. 732-31; when required to secure costs, sec. 619.*)

§ 38. 1. **Appointment of guardian *ad litem*—his duties and compensation.** No appointment of a guardian *ad litem* shall be made until the defendant is summoned, or until a person is summoned for him, as is authorized by section 52; nor until an affidavit of the plaintiff, or of his attorney, be filed in court, or with the clerk,

(8.) **Infant's real estate—sale of.** In action to sell infant's real estate the infant must be defended by a guardian *ad litem*. Girty v. Logan, 6 Bush 8.

(9.) **Consent order affecting infants.** Order of court made by consent, that infants be made parties does not dispense with service of process upon them, or authorize judgment against them without defense by a guardian. Pond v. Doneghy, 18 B. M. 558.

(10.) **Guardian should conduct defense.** Guardian, if he appears, has the right to conduct the defense for his wards, although a guardian *ad litem* may have been appointed, unless there is some valid reason for denying him the right. Walker v. Smyser, 80 Ky. 620; Miller v. Cabel, 81 Ky. 178. And the defense should be by the guardian if there is one, unless for good cause court appoints guardian *ad litem* to defend. The appointment of guardian *ad litem*, when there is a guardian is in the discretion of the court. McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226.

(11.) **Affidavit before appointment of.** While the Code requires an affidavit before the appointment of a guardian *ad litem*, the court held in McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226, that it was not a reversible error in that case to appoint a guardian *ad litem*, although no affidavit was filed.

(12.) **Answer by infants.** It would be an abuse of sound discretion to reject an answer offered by infants because not presented in time. Beverly v. Perkins, 1 Duv. 251.

§ 37. See note 2 to sec. 35.

§ 38. (1.) See notes 4, 6, 7, 10, 11 to sec. 36; and as to fees of guardian *ad litem* see Williamson v. Williamson, 1 Met. 303.

(2.) Guardian *ad litem* may prosecute an appeal in the name of and for the infants. Reed v. Bridge Co., 8 Bush 69.

classification.title: General Provisions Concerning Persons Under Disability
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the requirements for individuals to act as a 'next friend' in legal actions, detailing their residency and affidavit requirements. It also establishes the liability of guardians, curators, committees, or next friends for legal costs incurred during an action for a person under disability, unless they are permitted to sue in forma pauperis. Furthermore, it details the appointment, duties, and compensation of guardians ad litem, particularly in cases involving infants' real estate and consent orders affecting infants, emphasizing the need for defense by a guardian ad litem when a guardian is not actively conducting the defense.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with legal procedures for individuals under disability, such as minors or those deemed legally incapacitated. It specifies who can represent them in court and the responsibilities of those representatives. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest a racial basis for these legal procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also covers the form and requisites of a summons, and the ability to issue summons to any county.

The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1890.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code outlines the procedures for serving a summons on a defendant who is an infant under fourteen years of age or a person of unsound mind. For infants, service is directed to a father, guardian, mother, or person in charge, with specific provisions for cases where these individuals are plaintiffs. For persons of unsound mind, service must be on the individual and a committee, father, guardian, wife, or person in charge, with further stipulations for married women of unsound mind whose husbands are plaintiffs.

It specifies the order in which individuals (father, guardian, mother, wife, committee, person in charge) must be served. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to any individual falling under these categories, regardless of their race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1890.

§ 52 [81 ] . How served on infant under fourteen . If the
defendant be under the age of fourteen years the summons must be
served on his father ; or, if he have no father , on his guardian ; or,

Amendment ofJanuary 16 , 1882 : § 1. That if any of the parties
upon whom summons is directed to be served by section 52 of
the Civil Code is a plaintiff , then it shall be served on the person
who stands first in the order named in said section , and who is
not a plaintiff'; and if all such persons are plaintiffs , it shall , on
the affidavit of one or more of them showing that fact , be the
duty of the clerk of the court to appoint a guardian ad litem for
the infant , and the summons shall be served on such guardian .
§ 53 [82] . How served on person of unsound mind . If the
defendant be of unsound mind the summons must be served on him
and on one of the following named persons , if residing in the county ,
viz : on his committee ; or , if he have no committee , on his father ;
or, if he have no father , on his guardian ; or, if he have no guardian ,
on his wife ; or, if he have no wife , on the person having charge of
him ; or, if the defendant be a married woman of unsound mind , and
her husband be plaintiff in the action, the summons must be served
on her and her committee ; or , if her husband be not plaintiff in the
action , upon her and her committee, if she have one ; or , if she have
no committee , upon her and her husband : Provided, That the sum-
mons shall not be served upon a person of unsound mind , if he be
§ 52. (1. ) Infant-service on-presumption . In action against an infant
whose father was dead, the officer's return , showing that summons was executed upon
infant and his mother is sufficient , nothing appearing in the record to show he had a
guardian . It will be presumed officer did his duty. Webber v. Webber , 1 Met . 18 .
(2.) Return of special bailiff showing that he executed process upon infant and
his mother , without showing that father or guardian could not be found , is not suffi-
cient . Loyd v. McCauley , 14 B. M. 535. The court refrain from saying whether such
a return made by an officer would be sufficient . The Code of 1854 provided that if
neither the father nor guardian could be found , summons might be executed on
mother . 80 Ky. 64 .
(3. ) The return of sheriff on a summons against infant defendants , that he
executed it by delivering a copy to each of the persons named in it, negatives the
presumption that he served it on guardian or any one except those named in it.
Beverly v . Perkins , 1 Duv . 251 .

Amendment of January 16, 1882 : § 2. That section 53 of the
Civil Code of Practice be amended by adding at the end of said
section the following to -wit : " Service of a summons , by deliver-
ing a copy of it to the physician having charge of a person of
unsound mind , shall have the same effect as a service on the per-
son of unsound mind , if such physician gives a certificate ,
attested by the officer delivering him the copy , that a personal ser-
vice would , in his opinion , be injurious to such person of
unsound mind . " ( Venue of action against , sec . 69.)
§ 54 [83. ] How served on prisoner . If the defendant be a
prisoner a copy of the petition must accompany the summons , and
the service must be upon the keeper of the penitentiary , who shall
deliver the copies of the petition and summons to the prisoner . And
a copy of the summons must also be delivered to the curator of the
defendant, if he have one ; or, if he have no curator , to his wife , if
he have one ; or, if he have no wife , it shall be left at the place , if
known, where he resided, or claimed to reside , prior to his confine-
ment , with some person of the age of sixteen years . ( Venue of
action against , sec . 69.)
§ 55 [84 ] . How served on Shakers . If the defendant be a com-
munity of Shakers , holding property in common, the service must
be made by posting a copy of the summons at the door of its meet-
ing -house , and by delivering a copy thereof to some member of the
community .
§ 56 [86 ] . How served on non -resident . Excepting infants-
other than married women-- and persons of unsound mind and pris-
oners , if a defendant be out of this State the plaintiff may take a copy
of the petition , certified by the clerk , with a summons annexed
thereto , warning him to appear and answer the petition within sixty
days after the same shall have been served on him, and may cause a
copy thereof to be delivered to such defendant by a person to whom
he is personally known . Proof of the delivery shall be made by the
affidavit of the person making it, indorsed on or annexed to the certi-
fied copy and summons , in which the time and place of the delivery ,
§ 56. Personal judgment forbidden . Personal judgment can not be rendered
on service under this section . Griswold v. Popham , 1 Duv. 170 ; Berry v . Berry , 6
Bush 594 : sec . 419 .

Service of Summons- Constructive .
the action on the first day of the next term of the court , which
does not commence within sixty days after the making of the
order . ( Venue of action against , sec . 75 ; how defendant whose
name unknown described , sec . 691 ; form of warning order,

§ 58 [89] . 1. When warning order can be made-affidavit .
The clerk shall not make such warning order upon any of the grounds
mentioned in sub - sections 1 , 2, 3 , 4, and 7 , of section 57, except upon
an affidavit of the plaintiff' ; or of his agent or attorney , if he be
absent from the county ; or of his guardian , curator , committee , or
next friend , if he be under disability ; or of the attorney of either of
them , if absent from the county , stating the ground of the application
for such order .
2. Nor shall the clerk make such order on any of the grounds
mentioned in sub -sections 1, 2, and 4 , of section 57 , unless the
affidavit also state in what country the defendant, or , if the defend-
ant be a corporation , in what country its chief officer or agent ,
resides or may be found , and the name of the place wherein a
post- office is kept nearest to the place where the defendant or its
chief officer or agent resides or may be found ; or unless the affi-
davit state the affiant's ignorance of such of those facts as he does
not know .
3. Nor shall the clerk make such order upon an affidavit of an
other place , with no power to return , or respond to the warning order . Newcomb v.
Newcomb , 13 Bush 544 .
(6.) Strict compliance with Code necessary. In all proceedings upon con-
structive service , the provisions of the Code regulating the same must be literally
followed . Nothing short of a substantial compliance with every requisite will give
court jurisdiction . Brownfield v. Dyer , 7 Bush 505. See Jackson v. Speed , 2 Duv .
426; Grigsby v. Barr , 14 Bush 330 .
(7. ) Warning order . An order warning defendant to appear and answer on the
first day of a term commencing less than sixty days from date of order is absolutely
void . 7 Bush 505 .
§ 58. ( 1. ) Affidavit. Court of Appeals will not dismiss an appeal because of
defect in affidavit for warning order made in that court , but will quash the order if
affidavit is defective . Arthurs v. Harlan , 78 Ky . 138 .
(2. ) Affidavit for warning order must state that person against whom it is made is
a non -resident and believed to be absent from this State . 78 Ky . 138 .
(3. ) The absence of affidavit for warning order from the record does not render
the judgment void . The presumption is that the warning order was entered by the
court as directed in the Code . Newcomb v . Newcomb , 13 Bush 544 .
(4. ) Warning order-commencement of action . The taking of warning order
against non -resident defendant is commencement of action as to him ; no summons
is necessary . Hoffman v. Brungs , 83 Ky . 400 .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Service of Summons - Constructive
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the procedures for serving a summons constructively, particularly when the defendant is absent or under disability. It details the requirements for affidavits to be filed by the plaintiff, their agent, attorney, guardian, curator, committee, or next friend, and the conditions under which the clerk can issue a warning order. It also specifies the appointment and duties of an attorney for the defendant in such cases, along with provisions for compensation and the effect of the attorney's actions or inactions.

48 [TITLE IV .COMMENCEMENT OF A CIVIL ACTION .
Service of Summons - Constructive .
practicing attorney of the court ; but the court may , and for any
reason which it deems good , shall , appoint another attorney as a sub-
stitute for the attorney appointed by the clerk . Neither the plaintiff
nor his attorney shall be appointed, or be permitted to suggest the
name of, the defendant's attorney .
2. Such attorney must make diligent efforts to inform the
defendant, by mail , concerning the pendency and nature of the
action against him , and must report to the court, during the first
term, which does not commence within sixty days after his
appointment , the result of his efforts .
3. The duties and powers of such attorney are governed by the
last foregoing paragraph unless he be employed by the defendant ;
or unless he be unable to inform the defendant concerning the
action ; or unless he learn that the defendant is under disability ,
other than coverture , or other than infancy and coverture com-
bined.
4. Such attorney, if employed by the defendant, shall so report
to the court ; and such report shall be treated as an appearance
of the defendant in the action .
5. If such attorney can not inform the defendant concerning
the action , or if he learn that the defendant is under disability ,
other than coverture , or other than infancy and coverture com-
bined , he shall so report to the court , and shall make an affirm .
ative defense if he can ; or , if he can not make such defense , he
shall so report to the court , and shall be subject, with reference
to such report , to the provisions of sub -section 3 of section 36 :
and no act of his shall be treated as an appearance of such
defendant .
6. The provisions of sub - section 4 of section 38 apply with
reference to the compensation of such attorneys .
7. No judgment shall be rendered against such defendant if
under any disability , other than coverture , or infancy and cover-
ture combined, until a defense or report shall have been filed
(7. ) Judgment -attorney can not consent to. Attorney appointed for non-
resident has no authority to consent to judgment against him unless employed by
him ; and recital in judgment that it is rendered by consent will not sustain it .
Anderson v. Sutton , 2 Duv . 480 ; and see Ball v. Poor , 81 Ky . 26 .
(8. ) Answer enters appearance . Non -resident who by his attorney files an
answer sworn to by him in another State must be regarded as personally before the
court , although he disclaims in answer any intention of entering his appearance .
-Tipton v. Wright , 7 Bush 448 .

50 THE COUNTY IN WHICH AN ACTION MUST OR MAY BE BROUGHT . [TITLE
The County in which an Action Must or May be Brought .
§ 62 [93 ] . Concerning real property . Actions must be brought
in the county in which the subject of the action, or some part thereof,
is situated-
1. For the recovery of real property , or of an estate or
interest therein .
2. For the partition of real property except as is provided in
section 66 .
3. For the sale of real property under title 10, chapter 14, or
under a mortgage , lien , or other encumbrance or charge , except
for debts of a decedent .
4. For an injury to real property . (Definition of " real prop-
erty ," sec . 732-9 .)
§ 63 [94] . To recover a fine or forfeiture , or against public
officer . Actions must be brought in the county where the cause of
action , or some part thereof , arose-
must be construed together . Harris v. Adams , 2 Duv. 141. Secs . 57 , 60 , so far as
they regulate the time given for answer , do not apply to the Louisville chancery
court . 78 Ky . 79 .
(2. ) Depositions-guardian ad litem . Depositions taken upon notice to corre-
sponding attorney can be read against infant defendant constructively summoned ,
although he had no guardian ad litem . C. & L. R. R. Co. v . Bowler , 9 Bush 468. It
seems that this Code does not require a guardian ad litem for an infant non -resident .
See secs . 36 , 59 , sub-sec . 5. The Code of 1854 , sec . 55 , provided that no judgment
could be rendered against an infant until after defense for him by guardian .
§ 62. ( 1. ) Attachment may be levied on land in any county . Land may be
levied on and sold under an attachment in other county than one in which action is
pending in which attachment is obtained . Nixon v. Jack , 16 B M. 174. See also
Webb v. Wright , 2 Bush 126 , and sec . 201 .
(2. ) Land-to enforce lien on. Circuit court of one county has no jurisdiction
to enforce lien on land in another county . Webb v . Wright , 1 Bush 107 ; see same
case in 2 Bush 126 .
( 3.) Infant's land-jurisdiction to sell. Circuit court of one county has no
jurisdiction to sell land of infant in another county on petition of guardian of infant ,
although land descended to infant from his father , who died intestate in county
where suit brought , and his administrator qualified in that county . Montgomery v.
Montgomery , 2 Bush 49 .
(4. ) Title to quiet . Party having legal title and possession of land may institute
Suit against party claiming it, to quiet his title, in county where land lies . Landrum
v. Farmer , 7 Bush 46 .
(5. ) Title to perfect. Action to perfect title to land is transitory , and comes
under sec . 78. Page v. McKee , 3 Bush 135 .
$ 63. Sheriff-failing to return or collect execution . Action on sheriff's
bond for failing to return execution from another county , and failing to pay over
money collected on execution , must be brought in county where sheriff qualified .
Bank of Ky . v. Harrison , 1 Bush 384 ; Foster v. Wade , 4 Bush 628. And for failing
to make execution from another county , must be brought in county where he quali-
fied . Groom v. Picket, 4 Bush 372 .

classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the specific counties in which legal actions must or may be brought against various entities and individuals. It details venue requirements for actions against commissioners of the sinking fund or board of education, prisoners or inmates of asylums, and actions based on a return of no property found. It also addresses actions against banks and insurance companies, and provides case law interpretations for some of these provisions.

entries.ocr_text:
V.] THE COUNTY IN WHICH AN ACTION MUST OR MAY BE BROUGHT. 53

The County in which an Action Must or May be Brought.

sections 70 and 75, an action against an incorporated bank or insurance company may be brought in the county in which its principal office or place of business is situated; or, if it arise out of a transaction with an agent of such corporation, it may be brought in the county in which such transaction took place. (*Service of summons on, sec. 51-3.*)

§ 72 [101, 103]. **Against corporation.** Excepting the actions mentioned in sections 62 to 66, both inclusive, and in sections 68, 70, 71, 73, 75, and 77, an action against a corporation which has an office or place of business in this State, or a chief officer or agent residing in this State, must be brought in the county in which such office or place of business is situated, or in which such officer or agent resides; or, if it be upon a contract, in the above-named county, or in the county in which the contract is made or to be performed; or, if it be for a tort, in the first-named county, or the county in which the tort is committed. (*Who is "chief officer," sec. 732-33. Meaning of "resides" sec. 732-32. Service of summons on, sec. 51-3.*)

§ 73 [102]. **Against common carrier.** Excepting the actions mentioned in section 75, an action against a common carrier, whether a corporation or not, upon a contract to carry property, must be brought in the county in which the defendant, or either of several defendants, resides; or in which the contract is made; or in which the carrier agrees to deliver the property. An action against such carrier for an injury to a passenger, or to other person or his property, must be brought in the county in which the defendant, or either of several defendants, resides; or in which the plaintiff or his property is injured; or in which he resides if he reside in a county into which the carrier passes. (*Service of summons on, sec. 51-4.*)

§ 74. **For injury to person or character.** Every other action for an injury to the person of the plaintiff, and every action for an injury to the character of the plaintiff, against a defendant residing in this State, must be brought in the county in which the defendant resides, or in which the injury is done. (*See also, secs. 123-124.*)

§ 73. **Common carrier.** Action against common carrier whether it be a corporation or partnership may be brought in county where contract is made. Adams Ex. Co. v. Crenshaw, 78 Ky. 136.

§ 74. (1.) **False imprisonment.** Where warrant for arrest is sworn out in one county, and defendant is arrested in another, action for false imprisonment lies in county where he is arrested. Mitchell v. Ripy, 82 Ky. 516.

(2.) **Assault and battery.** Action may be brought in this State, for an assault and battery committed in another State. Watts v. Thomas, 2 Bibb 458.

54 THE COUNTY IN WHICH AN ACTION MUST OR MAY BE BROUGHT . [TITLE
The County in which an Action Must or May be Brought .
§ 75 [ 105 ] . Against person constructively summoned. Ex-
cepting the actions mentioned in sections 62 to 67 , both inclusive ,
and in section 70 and section 76, an action against a defendant who
may be proceeded against by a warning order , as is authorized by
section 57 , must be brought in a county in which the defendant
resides at the commencement of the action ; or in which he has
property ; or in which a person resides against whom he has a valid
claim for money or property .
§ 76. For alimony or divorce . An action for alimony or
divorce must be brought in the county where the wife usually resides ,
if she have an actual residence in this State ; if not , in the county of
the husband's residence . ( See also secs . 420 to 427.)
§ 77. Against contractor for public work . Excepting the
actions mentioned in section 75, an action against a contractor who
undertakes to construct , in whole or in part , any turnpike , bridge ,
railway , lock , dam, or other public work , for labor done , or materials
or supplies furnished , for the construction or promotion of said work ,
may be brought in the county wherein the labor, or most of it, is
done, or wherein the materials , or supplies, or most thereof, are
furnished ; and if such bridge connect two counties, such action may
be brought in either of them .
§ 78 [ 106] . Transitory actions . An action which is not required
by the foregoing sections of this article to be brought in some other
county may be brought in any county in which the defendant, or in
§ 75. Non-residents . If all the defendants are non -residents , action should be
brought in county in which one of them has property ; but if some of defendants are
residents of this State , it should be brought in county where one of them resides or
is summoned . Nixon v. Jack , 16 B. M. 174 .
§ 76. (1.) Divorce-Waiver of objection . By this statute (section ) the Legis-
lature only intended to fix the jurisdiction when it was disputed , or when defendant
was proceeded against by constructive service ; but if defendant is summoned and
appears and fails to object to jurisdiction, the court may grant the divorce , if it has
jurisdiction to grant one in any case . Johnson v . Johnson , 12 Bush 485. See as to
non -resident notes 3, 4, 5 to sec . 57 .
(2.) Residence -averment of. The allegation in petition that plaintiff " now
resides and for some while has resided in Garrard county " is prima facie equivalent
to the expression " usually resides " contained in this section .. Lochnane v. Loch-
nane , 78 Ky . 467. And allegation that plaintiff is resident of Campbell county , omit-
ting the word " Kentucky " is sufficient , Strode v. Strode , 3 Bush 227 .
§ 78. (1. ) Answer by one-effect on others . The answer of one of several
defendants not residing in county where suit is brought does not confer jurisdiction
as to other defendants who are summoned out of the county . Kennedy v. Daven-

classification.title: The County in which an Action Must or May be Brought
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the rules for determining the proper county in which to file various types of legal actions. It specifies where actions must be brought based on the defendant's residence, property ownership, or the location of a claim against them. It also details venue for actions concerning alimony or divorce, and for actions against contractors for public works. Transitory actions, not covered by other sections, can be brought in any county where the defendant resides or is summoned.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the rules of venue for civil and criminal actions in Kentucky as of 1890. It specifies where lawsuits can be filed based on factors like the defendant's residence, property, or the nature of the claim. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

(3.) Service on party improperly joined. A defendant improperly joined , and
summoned, will not give jurisdiction as to defendants residing in and summoned in
another county . Bayse v. Brown , 78 Ky . 553 ; Ransdall v. Shropshire , 4 Met. 327 .
(4.) Procured by fraud . Service upon defendant brought within jurisdiction of
court by fraud of plaintiff does not confer jurisdiction to render judgment . Wood v.
Wood, 78 Ky. 624 ; and defendant may show want of jurisdiction without entering
appearance . 83 Ky . 529 .
§ 79. ( 1. ) Service in another county. Suit was brought in county of defend-
ant's residence and process issued to that and another county, and was served in latter ,
but not in former , in time for judgment . It was error to render judgment on service
in another county . Raymond v. Reed , 16 B. M. 345 .
(2.) Defendant need not respond to summons in such case. Dyas v. Lindsay , 4
Bush 349 ; Ruby v. Grace , 2 Duv . 540.
(3. ) Evidence as to residence . Defendant sued in one county and served with
process in another ; evidence that he lived in former county when suit brought and
judgment rendered did not authorize jndgment ; but if he removed from former county
after suit filed, judgment would have been proper on evidence of that fact. Dyas v.
Lindsay, 5 Bush 506 .

when the action was begun, can not be summoned in that or any
other action of the plaintiff whilst visiting such county for the sole

action . If, after the commencement of an action in the county of

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 deals with the joinder of actions and rules regarding the jurisdiction and summoning of defendants, particularly when a defendant is summoned out of the county where the action is brought or moves after the action has commenced. It outlines conditions under which a judgment against one defendant might not affect another, especially concerning bankruptcy discharges, and specifies when a defendant cannot be summoned in a county for a separate action while visiting to defend a prior one. It also addresses the waiver of objections to jurisdiction.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details when a party can strike a cause of action from their pleading and when the court can require a party to elect which cause of action to pursue if multiple are improperly joined. Several case examples illustrate these principles, such as joining fraudulent vendees, actions against public officials, and contract disputes with fraud claims.

60 [TITLE VII .PLEADINGS IN CIVIL ACTIONS .
The Petition .
formed , sec . 114 ; when allegations confessed , sec . 126 ; when must
be proved , sec . 126-1 , 4 ; what writings must , or may , be filed , secs .
120 , 128 .
290. Requisites of petition .
CHAPTER II .
The Petition .
§ 90 [ 118 ] . Requisites of petition . The petition must state
facts which constitute a cause of action in favor of the plaintiff
§ 90. ( 1. ) Petition-provisions concerning . A pleading may contain as many
causes of action , legal and equitable , as there may be grounds for on behalf of the
pleader (sec . 113-2) ; but , if more than one , each must be stated in a separate , num-
bered paragraph (sec . 113-3) . The statements of the pleading must be consistent ,
and not inconsistent , with those of any other pleading previously filed by the same
party , unless he allege in the alternative the existence of one or another fact, and
state that one, but which one he does not know, is true (sec . 113-4 ) . The pleading
must be signed by the party or attorney (sec . 115), and need not state facts of which
judicial notice is taken , or the evidence (sec . 119-1) . Private statute, how pleaded
(sec . 119-2 ) , and judgment of court (sec . 122 ) .
(2. ) Parties -names of to be stated . The full names of all the parties , plaintiff
and defendant , and the fiducial or other character in which they sue , or are sued ,
should be stated in the body of the petition ; and, if partners , the full name of all
the members of the firm should be stated. Newman's P. and P. , pages 218 , 287 .
(3.) Forms , not substance , abolished . Forms have been abolished , but the
Code makes no change in the law which determines what facts constitute a cause of
action . The substance of the common law rules of legal procedure remains , and
every essential averment required to make a declaration good at common law must be
made in the petition . Hill v. Barrett , 14 B. M. 83 ; Richmond T. P. Co. v. Rogers , 7
Bush 532 ; L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy , 9 Bush 522 ; 6 Bush 532 .
(4. ) Allegation and proof. It is as necessary to state a cause of action as it is to
sustain it by proof . The absence of either prevents a recovery (Murrell v. McAlister,
79 Ky . 311 ), and the plaintiff can only recover upon proof of the cause of action
alleged in his petition . Gossom v. Badgett, 6 Bush 97 ; 1 Met. 339. As to variance
between pleading and proof, see secs . 129 , 130, 131, and notes thereto .
( 5. ) How pleadings construed . Before verdict , pleadings will be construed
against the pleader ; after verdict , pleadings will be liberally construed to uphold the
verdict , and a pleading insufficient before verdict may be good after verdict . Worthly
v. Hammond , 13 Bush 510. See Skillman v. Muir , 4 Met. 282 ; 16 B. Μ. 643 ; 10
Bush 185 .
( 6. ) Defects cured by answer-verdict . See note 2 to sec . 95 .
( 7.) Defense need not be anticipated . It is not necessary to notice and remove
objections that may exist and can be relied on for defense. L. & P. Canal Co. v.
Murphy , 9 Bush 522 ; Shelbyville Trustees v . S. & E. T. P. Co. , 1 Met . 54 ; Bentley
v. Bustard , 16 B. M. 643 ; 1 Met. 146 .
( 8. ) Facts-how to be averred . The facts relied upon should be directly and
positively alleged , and not stated by way of argument , inference or belief. Patterson
v. Caldwell , 1 Met . 489 ; Garnett v. Finnell , 2 Duv . 166 ; Walters v. Chinn, 1 Met.
499 ; 3 Met . 61. The failure to state a material fact can not be obviated by the state-
ment of another fact that raises a prima facie presumption of
itsexistence
. Meguiar
v. Henry
, 84 Ky
.
2;

. 59
; 11 Bush 693
.

It outlines what facts must be alleged to constitute a cause of action and what averments are insufficient.

(40. ) Dower . In action by widow against purchaser from her husband to have
dower allotted , the petition did not aver that her husband was the owner of the land
during coverture , nor that she was his wife when purchase was made by defendant ,
and was not good . Yancy v . Smith , 2 Met. 408 ; see Fritz v . Tudor , 2 Duv . 173 .
(41. ) Heir for debt of ancestor . To make the heir responsible for debt of
ancestor it must be alleged that he received assets from ancestor . Massie v. Hiatt ,
82 Ky. 314 ; 10 Bush 234 ; ib. 441 ; 80 Ky . 152. As to proper averment that one is heir ,
see 7 Bush 51 ; and 18 В. М. 72 .
(42. ) Vendor's lien to enforce. The petition must set forth the terms of the
contract , the character of title to be made, and that the vendor is able and willing to
convey according to the contract . Calvin v . Duncan , 12 Bush 101 ; Williams v.
Abrahams , 3 Bush 186 ; 79 Ky . 465 ; 78 Ky . 607.
(43. ) Petition should describe land sought to be subjected . Lawless v. Barger ,
9 Bush 665 ; see Runyon v. Darnall , 10 Bush 67 .
(44. ) Husband-for debt of wife. In action by husband to recover debt made
by wife before marriage , it is not necessary to allege that the husband received
property by the wife. Beaumont v. Miller , 1 Met. 68 ; Fultz v . Fox , 9 В. М. 499 .

recover damages caused by reason of it, if he allege and prove special injury to him-

action for trespass . Walden v. Conn , 84 Ky . 312 ; J. , M. & I. R. R. Co. v. Esterle, 13

66 [TITLE VII .PLEADINGS IN CIVIL ACTIONS .
The Demurrer .
1. That the court has no jurisdiction of the defendant , or of
the subject of the action ; or ,
2. That the plaintiff has not legal capacity to sue ; or,
3. That another action is pending , in this State, between the
same parties , for the same cause ; or ,
4. That there is a defect of parties, plaintiff or defendant .
Either of said grounds of objection , shown to exist by a plead-
ing, is waived , unless distinctly specified by a demurrer thereto ,
except the objection to the jurisdiction of the court of the sub-
ject of the action, which objection is not waived by failing so to
make it ; but a party failing so to make it when or before he files
a pleading , other than a demurrer , is liable for all costs resulting
from such failure . (May be filed in vacation , sec . 109 ; may be
made to part of pleading , sec . 113-1 ; ground of shown by plead-
ing , sec . 118. )
§ 93 [121 ] . 1. General defined-waiver of. A general demurrer
(2. ) Error as to proceeding . That action is brought in equity when it should
have been at law is not ground of demurrer . Trustees of Leb . v . Forrest , 15 В. М.
168 ; 14 Β. Μ. 348 .
( 3. ) Verification -misjoinder . Objection that petition not verified can not be
made by demurrer . Harris v. Ray, 15 B. M. 628. Nor can misjoinder of parties
be reached by demurrer . Dean v. English , 18 B. M. 132 .
(4. ) Jurisdiction . When court has no jurisdiction of subject matter , the error is
not waived by failing to demur . Hughey v. Sidwell , 18 B. M. 259 ; Fidler v. Hall , 2
Met . 461 ; Barton v. Barton , 80 Ky , 212. When the court has jurisdiction of the
subject matter , objection to the want of jurisdiction over the person may be waived .
Hughes v. Hardesty , 13 Bush 364 ; 4 Bush 619 ; 80 Ky . 212 .
( 5. ) Two actions pending . A. brought suit against B. in the Wayne circuit court
to recover property , and the action was transferred to Clinton county . While pending
in the court of latter county , A. brought suit in Wayne and obtained an attachment
for the same property . The petition showed that the action in Clinton was pending ,
and that the same property was sought to be recovered in each . A demurrer to
petition in Wayne was properly sustained . Moore v. Sheppard , 1 Met. 97. But
action to enforce lien and action on note secured by the lien may be prosecuted at
the same time . Julian v. Pilcher, 2 Duv . 254 .
(6. ) An action will not be abated on a plea that there is an action pending between
the same parties on the same debt in a court of another State . The Code only applies
to actions pending in this State . Davis v. Morton , 4 Bush 442 .
(7. ) Parties -defect of. In an action in the name of A. J. Morrison & Co. on a
note executed to A. J. Morrison & Co. , it does not appear from the petition whether
A. J. M. & Co. is one person or a firm , and demurrer will not lie . Morrison v. Tate ,
1 Met . 569. If defect does not appear in petition , it must be taken advantage of by
answer , or it will be waived . Sec . 118 ; 17 Β. Μ. 598 ; ib . 642 ; 3 Met. 134 ; 3 Bush 200 ;
80 Ky . 684 ; 10 Bush 763 ; and see notes to sec . 118 .
§ 93. ( 1.) Demurrer-petition how tested. On general demurrer to petition ,
its sufficiency should not be tested by blending with its averments any matter of
defense defendant may have . Yewell v . Bradshaw , 2 Duv . 573 .

classification.title: The Demurrer
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 defines a demurrer as an objection to a pleading that fails to state sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action or defense. It outlines the consequences of failing to make such an objection and explains the effect of a demurrer on previous pleadings, including how it brings all prior pleadings before the court. The text also details how a demurrer is treated when an answer is filed concurrently or subsequently, and addresses specific situations like misjoinder of causes of action, defects in form, and the sufficiency of relief sought. It clarifies that a judgment on a demurrer is not appealable unless a judgment follows it, and that a demurrer to a petition that doesn't state a cause of action is not a bar to future actions if new facts are alleged.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal concept and procedural rules surrounding a 'demurrer' in civil actions. It focuses entirely on the mechanics of legal pleadings and objections within the court system. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled 'Civil and Criminal Code' from 1890.

68 [TITLE VII .PLEADINGS IN CIVIL ACTIONS .
The Answer .
§ 94 [122] . Amendment after demurrer . If the court sustain
the demurrer , the party may amend the pleading , with leave of court ;
but in all cases he shall pay the costs resulting from the demurrer .
CHAPTER IV .
The Answer .
95. What the answer may contain .
4
96. Counter -claim-set-off-cross-petition-defined .
97. Proceedings upon counter -claims-cross -petitions-set-offs.
§ 95 [ 125 ] . What the answer may contain . An answer may
contain-
1. A traverse .
after setting forth a state of facts in his petition , whether sufficient or insufficient to
authorize a recovery after a general demurrer has been sustained to his pleading and
his action dismissed , to bring another action setting forth substantially the same
facts . Woolley v. Lou. Banking Co. , 81 Ky . 527 ; Birch v. Funk , 2 Met . 544 ; 81 Ky .
16 ; ib . 345 .
(16. ) Limitation . Demurrer to petition upon ground that action is barred by
limitation can not be sustained unless petition shows not only a sufficient lapse of
time , but the non - existence of any ground of avoidance . Rankin v. Turney, 2 Bush
555 ; Board v. Jolly , 5 Bush 86 ; Chiles v . Drake , 2 Met. 146. But when the petition
shows that the action is barred , and that plaintiff is not within any of the exceptions
contained in the statute which save his right to sue, a demurrer will be sustained .
Stillwell v . Leavy , 84 Ky . 379 .
(17. ) Statute of frauds . When petition shows want of necessary writing, it is
not necessary to plead statute of fraud ; question can be raised by demurrer . Linn
Boyd Co. v . Terrill , 13 Bush 463 .
§ 94. See notes to sec . 134 .
§ 95. ( 1.) Answer . Every material allegation of a pleading must , for the pur-
poses of the action , be taken as true , unless specifically traversed ( sec . 126) . A
material allegation is one which is necessary for the statement or support of a cause
of action or defense (sec . 127 ) . A traverse is a denial of facts alleged in an adverse
pleading . See sec . 113-7 .
(2.) Defects in petition cured by. Defective allegations in petition may be cured
and supplied by answer . Riggs v . Maltby, 2 Met. 88 ; Fible v. Caplinger , 18 B. м .
464 ; 9 Bush 522 ; 3 Bush 62 ; 16 Β. Μ. 643 ; 12 Bush 510 ; 78 Ky . 210 ; ib . 435 ; ib. 463 ; 13
Bush 601 ; ib. 681 ; 82 Ky. 291. But if the petition fail to allege matter which is essen-
tial to make out a cause of action , the defect will not be cured by the answer . Bogen-
schutz v . Smith , 84 Ky. 330. As to defects cured by verdict , see Worthly v. Ham-
mond , 13 Bush 510 ; L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522 ; 2 Met. 88 ; 10 Bush
185 ; ib . 169 ; ib . 87 ; 78 Ky . 463. But verdict will not cure failure to allege matter essen-
tial to make out a cause of action . Drake v . Semonin , 82 Ky . 291 ; 84 Ky . 830 ; and
see notes 1, 2, to sec . 326 .
(3. ) Denial of knowledge or information . Want of knowledge or information
sufficient to form a belief is not sufficient denial of the alleged presentation , demand ,
refusal of payment and protest of a bill, when the bill showing these facts is filed
with petition . Gridler v. F. and D. Bank , 12 Bush 333 ; ib. 287. Nor is denial that
note was duly protested , or that party received legal notice of protest , sufficient . 7
