Proceedings Upon Executions and Distress Warrants ..
administer to them an oath to make a fair appraisement of each arti-
cle of the property-whose appraisement, in writing, shall be recited
in the bond . (Form of appraisement , page 418. )
§ 647 [715 ] . To what court bond and appraisement returned .
Subject to the provisions of section 651 , the bond, with the appraise-
ment annexed thereto , shall be returned to the circuit court of the
county in which the levy was made .
§ 648 [716 ] . Party to whom bond executed may move for
judgment thereon. The party to whom the bond is executed may
move the court to which it is returned for a judgment thereon
against all or any of the obligors or their representatives , having
given to them ten days ' notice of the motion . The court shall direct
a jury to be impaneled, and may cause such issues to be tried as it
may prescribe , and direct which party shall be considered plaintiff
in the issues . If the property , or any part of it, be found subject
to the execution , judgment shall be rendered in favor of the plaintiff
therein for the value of the property so subject and ten per cent .
thereon , not exceeding the amount due on the execution and ten per
cent. thereon . An execution may be issued upon the judgment forth-
with , on which the same indorsement shall be made as on the execu-
tion in virtue of which the property had been seized . (See further as
to mode of proceeding , secs . 444 to 450.)
§ 649 [717 ] . Upon the trial either party may object to ap-
praisement . Upon the trial of the motion , either party may object
§ 648. ( 1.) Motion on bond-pleadings . The defendants in the motion may
be permitted by the court to file an answer . This Code , sec . 449 , permits written
pleadings in proceedings of this character . Couchman v. Maupin , 78 Ky. 33. Can
the motion be transferred to equity ? 78 Ky . 33.
(2. ) Effect of replevying the debt. When the defendants in the execution
replevy it after the execution of the claimant's bond, the obligors in the bond are
liable for the ten per cent . damages and the costs resulting from the execution of the
bond ; and if the replevin bond should be quashed , the plaintiffs in the execution
can proceed on the claimant's bond . Southern Bank v. White, 1 Duv. 290 .
(3.) Damages to be ascertained . A judgment ordering the payment of damages
by reference to the bond , without ascertaining and fixing the amount to be recovered ,
is erroneous . Smith v. Wells , 4 Bush 92 .
(4.) Notice-sufficiency of. In determining the sufficiency of the notice required
to be given before motion on the bond is made , it should be considered in connection
with the bond ( Smith v. Wells , 4 Bush 92 ) ; and when a party appears to a notice
defective in form , and , without objection , makes defense to the merits , he virtually
admits that he is fully informed of the claim asserted against him, and can not there-
after object to the sufficiency of the notice . Brown v. Gibson , 78 Ky . 602 .
(5.) See further , notes 1, 2, 3 , 4, to sec . 645 .

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for handling bonds and appraisements in cases of executions and distress warrants. It details the process of returning the bond and appraisement to the circuit court, the ability of the party to whom the bond is executed to move for judgment, and the trial process involving a jury to determine if property is subject to execution. It also specifies the judgment amount and the issuance of execution.

It outlines the administrative and judicial processes involved, including the role of bonds, appraisements, circuit courts, and juries. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described appear to be general legal mechanisms applicable to all parties involved in such financial disputes. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Proceedings Upon Executions and Distress Warrants
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for handling property claims made during executions and distress warrants. It details the process for bond requirements, the discharge of levies, and how proceedings are managed for executions from justices and quarterly courts, as well as distress warrants. It specifies where bonds should be returned and how motions regarding these bonds should be handled based on the value of the property in question.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details legal procedures related to property seizures and claims during legal proceedings like executions and distress warrants. It outlines the administrative and judicial steps involved in these processes, including the posting of bonds, the discharge of levies, and the jurisdiction of different courts (justices of the peace, quarterly courts, circuit court) based on property value. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and economic, related to debt collection and property rights. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
296 [TITLE XIV .MISCELLANEOUS PROCEEDINGS .
Proceedings Upon Executions and Distress Warrants .
ing bonds in such cases shall be returned with the warrants under
which they were taken . ( Tenant may sue plaintiff in warrant to
recover property taken by officer, sec . 33 ; form of distress warrant ,
page 419 ; provisions of General Statutes concerning , pages 367-374.)
§ 653 [721 ] . Tenant may discharge levy of distress warrant by
executing bond. If an officer levy or be about to levy a distress
warrant upon any property , the tenant, his assignee , or under - tenant
may execute , with one or more sufficient sureties , to be approved by
the officer, a bond to the party in whose favor the warrant issued , to
the effect that he will pay to such party the amount of the rent spec-.
ified in the warrant , with ten per cent . thereon , if the property be of .
the value of the rent so specified ; or , if it be of less value, that he
will pay to such party the value thereof, and ten per cent. thereon .
For the purpose of taking this bond the officer shall cause the prop-
erty to be appraised as is provided in section 646. The appraisement
shall be annexed and referred to in the bond . Upon the giving of
the bond , the levy , if one have been made , shall be discharged , and
the bond and warrant shall be returned to some justice of the peace
of the county , if the amount claimed do not exceed fifty dollars ; and ,
if it exceed that sum , to the clerk's office of the circuit court of the
county . (Form of bond and appraisement , page 418. )
§ 654 [722] . Plaintiff in distress warrant may move for judg-
ment on bond. The party to whom the bond is executed may move
the justice of the peace or the court for a judgment thereon against
all or any of the obligors or their representatives , having given to
them five days ' notice of the motion . The defendants may make
defense upon the ground that the distress was for rent not due in
whole or in part , or was otherwise illegal ; or , if the property was
levied upon , that it was by statute exempt from the levy ; and may
make any defense , by way of set - off or counter -claim , that is allowed
by the Code in actions .
§ 653. ( 1.) Estoppel-bond . A defendant in a distress warrant who executes,
the bond authorized by this section is estopped from denying that he is tenant ,
assignee or undertenant . Pegard v. Kellar , 4 Met . 260 .
(2. ) What bond to be executed . There is only one mode in which a tenant or
undertenant may discharge the levy of a distress warrant upon his property , and that
is by executing the bond provided for in this section . Where a tenant executed the
bond provided in sec . 645 , it could not be enforced by motion , did not discharge the
levy , and the officer had a right to retake and sell the property under the warrant .
Grubb v. McCoy, 2 Met . 486 .
( 3.) Distress warrant may be replevied . A distress for rent may be replevied .
Gen. Stat . , chap . 66 , art. 2, sec . 24. Dean v. Ball , 3 Bush 502. See page 374 .

classification.title: Proceedings Upon Executions and Distress Warrants
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process by which a tenant can discharge a distress warrant levied on their property by executing a bond. The bond guarantees payment of the rent owed, plus ten percent, or the value of the property if less than the rent. The statute also details the procedure for the plaintiff to obtain judgment on the bond and the defenses available to the defendant.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890, specifically concerning proceedings upon executions and distress warrants. The statute details the legal process for tenants to post bond to prevent the seizure of their property for unpaid rent. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Proceedings Upon Executions and Distress Warrants
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines procedures for trying motions related to executions and distress warrants. It details how juries are used to find facts in these cases, judgments for rent and damages, and how tenants can suspend distress warrants by executing bonds. It also abolishes jury trials for the right of property taken under execution or distress warrants and specifies the mode of proceeding if execution is levied on joint property, including provisions for partnership property.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details legal procedures related to property execution and distress warrants. It outlines how these legal processes are handled, including jury involvement, bond requirements, and the handling of joint or partnership property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and economic, not related to racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

CHAPTER VII .
Proceedings by Sureties .
2661. After debt matures surety may sue principal or co-surety .
662. Mode of proceeding-may obtain provisional remedies .
§ 661 [728 ] . After debt matures surety may sue principal or
co-surety . After the maturity of a debt or liability , an equitable
action may be brought by a surety against his principal to compel
payment of it, or by one who is jointly liable therefor with another
to compel him to pay so much of it as he may be equitably liable for
as between him and the plaintiff . (Action before debt due , secs . 237 to
248 ; when may proceed by motion , sec . 444.)
§ 662 [730] . 1. Mode of proceeding -may obtain provisional
remedies . The creditor must be made defendant to an action
brought pursuant to section 661 , but shall not be liable for costs .
§ 661. ( 1. ) Indorser against prior indorser . An action by the second in-
dorser of a bill of exchange against the first indorser is substantially an action by a
surety against his principal for indemnity . Scott v. Doneghy , 17 B. M. 321.
( 2. ) Surety against co-surety-limitation. A surety against whom a judgment
is obtained , and who pays the debt , can not require his co-surety to contribute , if at
the rendition of the judgment an action on the note against the co-surety would be
barred by limitation . Shelton v. Farmer , 9 Bush 314 ; Cochran v . Walker , 82 Ky . 220.
(3.) Payment by surety-what is. When one surety executes his note to the
creditor in discharge of their obligation , his cause of action against his co-surety for
contribution accrues . Stubbins v. Mitchell , 82 Ky . 535 .
(4.) Insolvency of principal. An action can not be maintained by one surety
against a co-surety to recover money paid for their principal unless the principal is
insolvent . Bolling v. Doneghy , 1 Duv. 220 ; Lee v. Forman , 3 Met. 114 .

classification.title: Proceedings by Sureties
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines legal proceedings related to sureties. It details how a surety can sue their principal or co-surety after a debt matures to compel payment or contribution. It also describes provisional remedies available to the surety, including making the creditor a defendant in the action. The text includes case law annotations related to indorsers and co-sureties.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 deals with legal proceedings involving sureties, principals, and co-sureties. It outlines the conditions under which a surety can sue to compel payment of a debt or seek contribution. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply generally to any individuals in a surety relationship, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text clearly pertains to civil procedure and has no racial implications.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Process Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general provisions related to legal process, including when process can be issued (not before petition filing), and that process can be issued and executed on holidays or Sundays. It also details specific modifications to general provisions for certain actions and clarifies what constitutes 'process' and 'writ'.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for the issuance and execution of legal process within the Kentucky Civil and Criminal Code of 1890. It focuses on the timing of legal actions, the validity of documents issued on holidays or Sundays, and the roles of court officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

Process .

§ 666 [736 ] . When defendant privileged from service of
process . Subject to the provisions of section 542, the defendant shall
have no privilege of exemption from the service of the process men-
tioned in section 665 , except from an arrest, by reason of his attend-
ance at any muster, election, or order of survey , or as a witness at
any court or other place .
§ 667 [ 66 , 737] . 1. To what officers process may be directed .
Every process in an action or proceeding shall be directed to the
sheriff of the county ; or, if he be a party , or be interested, to the
coroner ; or, if he be interested , to the jailer ; or, if all these officers
be interested , to any constable .
2. The summons or an order for a provisional remedy , in an
action or proceeding, may, at the request of the party in whose
behalf it is issued , be directed to any of the officers named in the
first sub - section who is not a party to nor interested in the action .
Act of March 10, 1880 : Elisor is authorized to " execute sum-
mons , subpœnas , and process in provisional remedies ."
§ 668 [ 738 ] . Court may appoint person to execute. The court ,
for good cause , may appoint a person to serve a particular process or
order , and he shall have the same power to execute it which a sheriff
has . His return must be verified by his affidavit . He shall be
entitled to the fees allowed to sheriffs for similar services .
§ 667. ( 1. ) Summons-service of by officer interested void. Judgment in
an action in which the sheriff is plaintiff, although not pecuniarily interested , upon
summons executed by him is void . Knott v. Jarboe, 1 Met . 504.
( 2. ) Attachment -by whom to be executed . An attachment must be executed
by the officer to whom it is directed , and can not , like a summons , be executed by an
officer to whom it might have been directed . Menderson v. Specker , 79 Ky . 509 .
(3.) Summons-who may execute. A summons directed to the sheriff may be
executed by a constable , or any officer to whom it might have been directed . Sec.
47; Long v. Gaines , 4 Bush 353 .
(4. ) Plaintiff's right to have process directed . The plaintiff has an unlimited
right to have his original process directed at his option to any of the officers authorized
to execute it. Boaz v. Nail , 2 Met . 245.
(5. ) Presumption that officer did his duty. It will be presumed that an officer
did his duty, and executed process as the law requires it to be done , unless the
process shows that he did not. Webber v. Webber , 1 Met. 18 ; Case v. Colston , 1
Met . 145 ; Lewis v. Quinker , 2 Met. 284 ; Anderson v . Sutton , 2 Duv. 480 ; Thomas v.
Mahone, 9 Bush 111. But this presumption does not arise when the process has
been executed by a special bailiff. Loyd v . McCawley , 14 B. M. 535 .
(6. ) Deputy-execution of process by. See notes to sec . 678 .

It details when defendants are privileged from service, to whom process can be directed (sheriff, coroner, jailer, constable, or an appointed person), and clarifies rules regarding the execution of summons and attachments by various officers. It also includes case law annotations related to these provisions.

It specifies which officials can serve different types of legal documents and under what circumstances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Duties of Clerks
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the general duties of clerks in legal proceedings. It details requirements for endorsing the time of filing papers and returning bonds, entering summons returns on the docket, administering oaths, taking affidavits, and making orders of survey. It also includes case law annotations related to deputy clerks, the evidentiary effect of summons return entries, errors in fixing return days for executions, the rights and duties of clerks regarding transcripts, liability for negligence, and the authorization to receive money.

It covers the handling of legal documents, the recording of court actions, and the authority of clerks to perform certain judicial functions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1890.

a majority of them concurring .
§ 680 [751 ] . Oath may be substituted by affirmation . An
oath required by this Code may be substituted by the affirmation of a
person who is conscientiously opposed to taking an oath .
§ 681 [752] . Mode of counting days between acts. If a cer-
tain number of days be required to intervene between two acts , the
day of one only of the acts may be counted .
§ 682 [753 ] . New bond may be executed for defective one. If
a bond provided for by this Code be adjudged to be defective , a new
and sufficient one may be executed in such reasonable time as the
court may fix, with the same effect as if originally executed .
§ 683 [ 754 ] . Officer may swear surety as to his sufficiency .
An officer whose duty it is to take a surety in any bond provided for
by this Code may require the person offered as surety to make affi-
davit of his qualifications , which affidavit may be made before such
officer . The taking of such an affidavit shall not exempt the officer
from any liability to which he might otherwise be subject for taking
insufficient security . (Form of affidavit , page 407.)
§ 684 [755 ] . Qualifications of sureties. The surety in every
bond provided for by this Code must be a resident of this State , and
§ 681. Time-how computed . 'The rule is well settled that where the com-
putation is to be made from the act done then the day in which it is done must be
included , but if to be made after or from the day itself, the day must be excluded ."
Moar v. Cov . Bank , 80 Ky . 305 ; Chiles v . Smith , 13 B. M. 461 ; Handley v. Cunning-
ham , 12 Bush 401 ; Wood v . Com ., 11 Bush 220 .
§ 682. ( 1. ) Defective bond . A traverse bond being defective in limiting the
cost and damage to which the traverser might be subjected to $ 100, the court should
have permitted a new bond to be executed . Alderson v . Trent, 79 Ky . 259.
(2. ) Appeal bond. If a party desiring to appeal attempts in good faith to exe-
cute a bond , and does execute such a bond as is prepared for him by the clerk and it
is defective , he should be allowed to execute a new bond . Adams v. Settles , 2 Duv .
76 ; Walters v . Patrick , 1 Bush 223 ; Manier v. Lindsey , 3 Bush 94 .
(3. ) Where the appellant and his surety signed their names to a blank paper , with
authority to the clerk to write their names above the signatures , which he failed to do ,
the appeal was properly dismissed ; a new bond could not be executed . Hargis v.
Pearce , 7 Bush 234 .
(4. ) Attachment bond. An attachment bond not taken by the clerk nor in the
manner prescribed is not a defective bond that can be remedied or substituted by a
new one . Horne v . Mitchell , 7 Bush 181 .

1. He shall be described in the petition and process as

§ 692. In action to enforce lien on property other liens on
must be stated . The plaintiff in an action for enforcing a lien on
property shall state in his petition the liens held thereon by others ,
making them defendants ; and may ask for and obtain a judgment
for a sale of the property to satisfy all of said liens which are
shown to exist , though the defendants fail to assert their claims .
Such defendants shall not , however , be allowed to withdraw or
receive any of the proceeds of such sale , until they have shown their
right thereto by answer and cross -petition . But, unless a personal
judgment be prayed for in such cross -petition , there need not be any
summons thereon ; and it shall be treated, with reference to the time
of answering thereto , as a set - off or counter -claim .
§ 693. Proceedings against surety after bankruptcy of prin-
cipal. In an action for the recovery of money in which the defend-
ant may have given bond with sureties , or for whom sureties may
have given bond , pursuant to the provisions of sections 14 , 214 , 221 , or
258 ; or in which an appellant may have given bond with sureties , or
for whom sureties may have given bond , pursuant to the provisions
of section 724 or section 748-if such defendant or appellant have
thereafter become a bankrupt under the laws of the United States ,
upon notice thereof from his adversary being served on the sureties ,
pursuant to the provisions of chapter 2 of title 4, concerning the ser-
vice of a summons, the court shall decide whether or not anything ,
and, if anthing , how much , should have been adjudged against the
defendant or appellant , but for the bankruptcy ; and such decision
shall be conclusive as to the liability of the sureties ; but they shall
not be liable upon a bond given pursuant to section 214 , if property

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890, Section 694
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines procedures for the sale of real property in legal actions, particularly when dealing with debts and liens. It details how courts should determine if property can be divided without impairing its value, when the entire property must be sold, and how proceeds should be distributed. It also addresses situations involving multiple liens and vendor's liens, including when notes are not yet due.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the legal procedures for selling real property to satisfy debts and liens. It focuses on the divisibility of property, the sale of the whole parcel if indivisible, and the distribution of proceeds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1890.

308 [TITLE XV .GENERAL PROVISIONS .
Miscellaneous Provisions .
petition or otherwise , that several debts are secured by one lien ,
or by liens of equal rank, and they are all due at the commence-
ment of the action , or become so before judgment , the court shall
order the sale for the pro rata satisfaction of all of them ; but ,
if, in such case , the debts be owned by different persons and be
not all due , the court shall not order a sale of the property until
they all mature . If all such liens be held by the same party , the
court may order a sale of enough of the property to pay the
debts then due, unless it appear that it is not susceptible of
advantageous division ; or, that , for some other reason , the sale
would cause a sacrifice thereof, or seriously prejudice the inter-
ests of the defendants .
§ 695. Whole of indivisible personalty may be sold-payment
of excess . If it be necessary to sell, for payment of debt , one or
more parcels of personal property which , in the opinion of the officer
making the sale , can not be divided without materially impairing the
value thereof, he shall sell the whole of said property , though it bring
more than the sum to be raised ; but he shall have no commission on
the excess . The excess shall be paid to the defendant , or the bond
therefor shall be payable to him .
§ 696. Time , place and terms of sale of property . Every
sale made under an order of court must be public , upon reasonable
§ 696. (1.) Sale-duty of commissioner . In advertising the land and conduct-
ing the sale, the commissioner should follow the directions of the judgment . Jarboe
v . Colvin , 4 Bush 70 ; Hahn v. Pindell , 1 Bush 538 .
(2.) The judgment failed to designate the place of sale , and the commissioner
sold the property at a place some miles from the court -house . The sale having been
reported and confirmed was not void , however erroneous it might have been . Revill
v. Claxon , 12 Bush 558. See Dawson v. Litsey , 10 Bush 408 ; Doughty v. Moss , 1
Bush 161 ; Perry v. Seitz , 2 Duv . 122 ; Dunn v. Salter , 1 Duv . 342 .
(3.) Commissioner may employ an auctioneer to cry the sale, but the sale must be
made under the immediate direction of and in the presence of the commissioner .
Noland v. Noland, 12 Bush 426 .
(4. ) Judgment should describe property . The judgment should describe the
property to be sold , so that the commissioner may know from it what property he is
directed to sell . Faught v. Henry, 13 Bush 471 ; Posey v . Green , 78 Ky. 162 ; Runyon
v. Darnall, 10 Bush 67 ; 9 Bush 665 ; 12 Bush 426 .
(5. ) Purchaser . A purchaser can not have a sale set aside because the commis-
sioner did not advertise the property as required by law, Watson v. Violett , 2 Duv .
332 ; nor because the land is not sufficiently described in the judgment . Sullivan v.
Berry , 83 Ky . 198 .
(6. ) A purchaser at commissioner's sale excepted to the confirmation of the report
of sale on the ground that there had been puffing and by-bidding at the sale, but was
held to have waived his right to object by executing sale bonds after obtaining knowl-
edge of all the facts connected with the sale. Robinson v. Robinson , 11 Bush 176 .

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines procedures for the sale of property when multiple debts are secured by a single lien or liens of equal rank. It details how the court should order sales for pro rata satisfaction of debts, especially when debts are owned by different persons and not all are due. It also addresses the sale of indivisible personal property and specifies requirements for the time, place, and terms of property sales conducted under court order, including the duties of a commissioner and considerations for purchasers. The text includes case law annotations related to these provisions.

The text also covers the sale of indivisible personal property and the requirements for public sales. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to be general legal procedures applicable to all citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Miscellaneous Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 details provisions related to judicial sales of property. It outlines requirements for the bond of the purchaser, conditions under which sales can be set aside (e.g., fraud, inadequacy of price, irregularities), and the powers of the court and commissioner regarding these sales. It also specifies notice requirements and the location of sales.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 and discusses the procedures and conditions for judicial sales of property. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural regarding property sales.

entries.ocr_text:
310 [TITLE XV .GENERAL PROVISIONS .
Miscellaneous Provisions .
for the price , with good surety approved by the officer making the
sale , payable to him or to the person entitled to receive the money ,
as the court may direct ; or, if the court make no order on the sub
ject, they shall be made payable to the officer .
2. They shall bear interest from date at the rate the judgment
bears .
3. They shall have the force of judgments ; and on executions
issued upon them no replevy shall be allowed , and sales shall be
for cash . (Form of bond , page 405.)
§ 698. When purchaser of personalty entitled to it. The
purchaser of personal property sold under an order of court shall be
entitled to it, upon complying with the terms of sale .
§ 699. Lien retained on real property sold-how released .
A lien shall exist on real property sold under an order of court , as
security for the purchase money ; and, upon payment thereof, the
clerk shall release the lien on the margin of the record of the deed
in the office of the clerk of the county court .
accepting the purchaser's bouds demand additional security . If the bond is insuffi-
cient, that fact should be brought to the attention of the court , and the court may
require additional security before confirming the sale . Reamer v.Judalı , 13 Bush 206 .
(2. ) Mistake in quantity of land sold-relief for. Where there is a mistake
as to the quantity of land sold at a judicial sale, the chancellor will in a subsequent
action afford relief, if the mistake be such that relief would be granted if the sale had
been a private one . Miller v. Craig , 83 Ky . 623 .
( 3. ) Purchaser at void sale . A purchaser at a sale made under a void judgment
does not acquire any lien on the property sold , for the amount of the debt paid by
him when the sale is set aside . Grigsby v. Barr , 14 Bush 330 .
(4.) Rents-right of purchaser to . A purchaser at a judicial sale is entitled to
rent from the date of the confirmation of the sale. Ball v. Bank , 80 Ky . 501 ; Talia-
ferro v . Gay , 78 Ky . 496 .
(5. ) Reversal - effect of. The reversal of a judgment under which a sale is
made does not affect the title of the purchaser at the sale, although he may be the
plaintiff. The sale can not be quashed . When the sale is confirmed the chancellor
loses all control over it , except during the term at which the order is made, and the
only remedy of the party complaining is to except to the confirmation , if there is
sufficient cause , and if the exceptions are overruled to appeal. Yocum v. Forenman ,
14 Bush 494 ; and see Vanbussum v. Maloney , 2 Met. 550 .
(6. ) Title-no warranty of. There is no warranty of title in judicial sales , and
after the sale has been confirmed , and no fraud practiced on the purchaser by the
creditor to induce the purchase , or warranty of title , no relief will be granted and no
deduction made from the purchase price because of liens on the land which the
purchaser will be compelled to pay . If, before the sale is confirmed , or during the
term at which it is confirmed , the purchaser discovers a defect in the title and applies
to the chancellor he will be granted relief. Farmers ' Bank v. Peters , 13 Bush 591 .
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1890, p. 321

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890, Section 698-699
classification.summary: These sections of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky detail the rights of purchasers of personal and real property sold under court order. For personal property, the purchaser is entitled to it upon complying with the terms of sale. For real property, a lien is retained as security for the purchase money, which can be released by the clerk upon payment. The text also includes case law annotations regarding issues like insufficient bonds, mistakes in land quantity, purchasers at void sales, rights to rent, effects of judgment reversal, and warranty of title in judicial sales.

classification.title: Mode of Proceeding in Inferior Courts
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the general procedures for various inferior courts in Kentucky, including quarterly courts, police courts, city courts, mayors' courts, and courts of justices of the peace. It details how process is directed, the duties of judges and clerks, and the general conduct of proceedings within these courts. It also references other sections of the code that regulate proceedings and specifies that land cannot be levied on under execution from these courts.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from Title XVI, Chapter 1 of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky. It details the procedural rules for various inferior courts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute focuses solely on the legal processes and jurisdiction of these courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

312 [TITLE XVI .INFERIOR COURTS .
Mode of Proceeding in .
courts , shall be directed to the marshal or a constable ; and those from
courts of justices of the peace shall be directed to a constable ; or to
a special agent appointed by the justice , by an indorsement on the
process, upon an affidavit of the plaintiff or his agent being filed
before the justice , to the effect that the process can not be executed ,
according to the belief of the affiant, unless such special agent be
appointed : but such process may be served by any officer or person
who is authorized by this Code to serve a summons . (Officers who
can serve summons , sec . 667 ; form of summons , page 402 ; of subpæna ,
page 421 ; of execution , page 416 ; of distress warrant page 419. of at-
tachment , page 412.)
§ 702 [824] . Duties and powers of judge or justice as clerk .
The duties and powers enjoined and conferred by this Code upon
clerks shall devolve on the judges or justices who are by law required
to act as clerks of their courts .
§ 703 [825 ] . 1. Clerk of quarterly court-duties of county
clerk. The presiding judge of the quarterly court shall be the clerk
of such court .
2. The county judge may, by an order entered of record in the
quarterly court , empower the county clerk to act as clerk of the
quarterly court ; and, upon such order being entered , the clerk
shall be authorized to issue any process of the quarterly court ,
as fully as the judge might do , acting as clerk of such court .
And the county clerk issuing such process shall be entitled to
the same fees, and subject to the same responsibilities , as the
judge would have been .
3. Such order shall not prevent the judge from acting as clerk
of the quarterly court in any case in which he may choose so
to act .
4. If no such order be made , the county clerk shall , in the
absence of the county judge from his office , act as clerk of the
quarterly court , in the same manner as if such order had been
made .
5. The county clerk shall, at the commencement of each term
of the quarterly court , deliver to the presiding judge all process
of such court returned to him, and all papers filed with him
relating to actions pending in such court .
§ 704 [ 826 ] . Only one docket to be kept . Only one docket is
required to be kept-on which shall be entered all the actions and
motions as they shall be returned .

§ 705 [827 ] . When pleadings may be oral. If the matter in
controversy do not exceed fifty dollars , the pleadings in the action
may be oral and without verification . But before the summons is
issued the plaintiff shall file in the court the account , or the written
contract , or a short written statement of the facts , on which the
action is founded . ( The forms of pleadings commencing on page 385
will answer in cases in quarterly and justices ' courts when written
pleadings are required .)
§ 706 [828] . Time in which summons must be served to
authorize trial . In the actions named in the last section, the ser-
vice of the summons five days before the time of trial shall authorize
a trial and judgment . If the matter in controversy exceed fifty dol-
lars, the service of the summons ten days before its return day shall
authorize a trial and judgment , whether the proceedings are ordinary
or equitable . (Forms of judgments , page 423. )
§ 707 [ 829 ] . Proceedings when action called for trial . Upon
the calling of an action for trial, if the summons have been served in
due time , the parties must proceed forthwith to form an issue and try
the case , unless the court give further time, or grant a continuance .
§ 708 [830 ] . How evidence taken in equitable actions . In
equitable actions, the parties may either take the depositions of wit-
nesses , or examine them orally in court .
§ 709 [831 ] . How subpœna served. A subpœna may be served
by showing it to the witness and informing him of the substance of it ,
without delivering to him a copy or ticket .
§ 710 [832] . District in which action to be tried. If the
defendant reside in the county in which the summons is issued, it
shall be returned for trial in the district of his residence , unless he
otherwise consent in writing , signed by him and indorsed on the
summons ; or, unless the justices in such district be interested or

314 [TITLE XVI .INFERIOR COURTS .
Mode of Proceeding in .
refuse to act , in which case it shall be returned in an adjoining dis-
trict . If it be issued by the judge of the quarterly court , it shall be
returned before him , unless the defendant, or defendants , all reside
in one district and there be an acting justice of the peace and an act-
ing constable in such district .
The amendment of February 23 , 1882 , striking out the following
words in this section to-wit : " Unless the defendant or defend-
ants all reside in one district , and there be an acting justice
of the peace and an acting constable in such district ," was re-
pealed by act of February 21 , 1884. This section has been amended
so far as applicable to the counties of Bath , Bourbon and Mercer .
§ 711 [833 ] . Place of trial if more than one defendant. If
there be two or more defendants residing in the county , but in differ-
ent districts , the summons shall be returnable in either district .
§ 712 [ 834 ] . Place of trial in action against transient defend-
ant. If a defendant transiently in a county be served with a sum-
mons issuing in said county , it shall be returned in the district in
which he is served , unless he otherwise consent in writing signed by
him .
§ 713 [835] . When jury trial may be demanded . The parties
shall not be entitled to a trial by jury, unless the matter in contro-
versy exceed in value twenty dollars . Upon a jury trial being
claimed, if it be allowable, the presiding judge or justice shall order
a jury to be forthwith impaneled to try the action . The jurors shall
be qualified as is required in the circuit courts .
§ 714 [836 ] . Time in which new trial may be granted . A new
trial may be granted in the quarterly courts or courts of justices of
the peace , upon motion made within ten days after a judgment has
been rendered, of which motion reasonable notice shall be given to
the adverse party .
§ 715 [837 ] . Justice to keep record of proceedings . Each
presiding judge and justice of the peace shall , in a book provided for
that purpose, keep a full and fair record of judicial proceedings in his
court ; and keep a good cross - index of the names of the litigants , refer-
ring to the pages of the book of every entry in each case .
§ 716 [ 839 ] . When interrogatories may be filed to be an-
swered by adverse party . Either party may file with the judge,
justice , or clerk of the court , in which the case is to be tried , written
interrogatories , to be answered by an adverse party who resides out
of the county where the court sits , or who is unable to attend court

§ 722 [845] . Land can not be levied on under execution from .
Land shall not be sold nor levied on under execution from a quarterly
or justice's court , or any court of similar jurisdiction .
§ 723 [846 ] . Proceedings to sell land when execution re-
turned no property found . When an execution upon a judg-
ment of a court mentioned in section 722 is returned by a proper
officer, in whole or in part , in substance , no property found to
satisfy it , the judge or justice of the court shall , upon application
of the plaintiff or his attorney , furnish a certified copy of the judg-
ment , execution and return ; which , on being filed in the office of the
clerk of the circuit court of the county in which the judgment was
rendered , shall be recorded by him in a book kept for that purpose ;
and, thereupon , the plaintiff shall be entitled to the same remedies
for the amount of his judgment and costs , including the costs of said
copy and of recording it, as if the judgment had been rendered in
the circuit court .

his judgment by execution , but can not sell the attached property . If the circuit
court sustains the attachment an order will be made directing a sale of the attached
property . Hawkins v. Baldauf , 10 Bush 624 .
( 3.) Jurisdiction -waiver. Where the circuit court has jurisdiction of the sub-
ject matter , if an appeal is taken to it, when it should have been taken to the
quarterly court, the objection to the jurisdiction will be waived by failure to move
to dismiss the appeal and consenting to the trial. Hughes v . Hardesty , 13 Bush 364 ;
seeFidler v. Hall , 2 Met. 461 .
(4. ) Jurisdiction -consent can not give . A case appealed from the justice's
court to the quarterly court can not , without any trial there , be transferred by agree-
ment to the circuit court . The latter court should dismiss the appeal . Davis v.
Davis , 10 Bush 274. And where the justice dismissed a warrant because the amount
in controversy exceeded his jurisdiction , and the quarterly dismissed the case on
appeal to that court , the plaintiff having appealed to the circuit court , that court
should have dismissed the appeal for want of jurisdiction . Fidler v . Hall , 2
Met. 461.
(5.) Amount in controversy . In determining the amount in controversy the
Interest is always computed up to the time when the amount in controversy is neces-
sary to confer jurisdiction . Orth v. Clutz , 18 B. M. 223 ; Fidler v. Hall , 2 Met. 461 ;
Bracy v . Bracy , 12 Bush 153 ; Wigginton v. Moss , 2 Met. 38.

classification.title: Appeals from Inferior Courts
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the procedures for appeals from judgments of inferior courts. It details requirements for appeal bonds, the process of staying proceedings, and the transmission of original papers. It also discusses jurisdictional issues, including waiver of objections and the determination of the amount in controversy for appeal purposes. Several case citations are included to illustrate specific legal points.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 and details the legal procedures for appeals from inferior courts. It focuses on procedural aspects like bond requirements, staying judgments, and jurisdictional rules. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

direct the appeal to be taken to the circuit court , and the judge
or justice shall make a minute thereof as part of the record .
(Form of summons on appeal , page 403 ; appeal bond and super-
sedeas , page 426 ; if appeal bond defective, new one may be
executed , sec . 682 ; as to jurisdiction of appeals , see page 375.)
725 [848 ] . Original papers to be transmitted to court ap-
pealed to. The judge , mayor , or justice , on being served with said
order , shall , in ten days thereafter , transmit all the original papers in
the action to the clerk's office of the court to which the appeal is
taken.
§ 726 [849 ] . When appeals stand for trial-how tried. Ap-
peals shall be docketed and stand for trial as ordinary actions, and
shall be tried anew , as if no judgment had been rendered .
§ 726. ( 1.) Amendment on appeal-garnishee . A. filed an affidavit against
B. in a justice's court, and garnisheed $59 in the hands of C. No petition was filed.
The attachment was discharged , and C. directed by the justice to pay the money
attached to B. , which he did. A. prayed an appeal at the time the attachment was
discharged , and the same day filed his appeal in the circuit court. On the trial in
the circuit court A. was permitted to file an amended petition , setting up properly
his claim . The attachment was sustained , and the garnishee required to pay the
money attached to A. This judgment was affirmed . Whether , when money is
attached , the garnishee must hold it until the time to appeal expires not decided .
Puff v. Hutcher, 78 Ky . 146 .
( 2.) Power of circuit court on appeal. The only power the circuit court has on
appeal is to render judgment for whatever may be found due on the trial ; or, if
nothing is found due , to dismiss the action ; or, if the appeal is not regularly
prosecuted , or the court has no jurisdiction , to dismiss the appeal , and until some of
these things are done the case remains in court . The court has no power to reverse
the judgment of the justice . Bennett v. Thompson , 10 Bush 365 .
( 3. ) When an appeal is taken to the circuit court from the judgment of a justice's
court that had no jurisdiction of the subject matter , no recovery can be had in the
circuit court against the defendant , and in such case the circuit court should make
such orders as will render the proceedings in the justice's court inoperative . Wig-
ginton v. Moss , 2 Met. 38 .
(4. ) If, on an appeal from the judgment of a justice, it appears that the justice
had no jurisdiction of the cause, the court should dismiss the action . Fleming v.
Limebaugh , 2 Met. 265.
(5.) Same case to be tried on appeal . Upon the trial of an appeal the cause of
action must be the same as that expressed in the warrant , and it must be a cause of
action over which the justice had jurisdiction . It must be the same identical case
which was tried by the justice, and not a new case. Upon the trial of the appeal no
new cause of action can be relied upon . The same action may be tried anew, but no
new action is to be tried . Burbage v. Squires , 3 Met . 78 .
(6.) Amendment of answer . "A plaintiff can not, upon an appeal , amend his
pleadings so as to set up a new and independent cause of action . The case, i. e., the

§ 734 [876] . By whom and how appeal taken. The mode of
bringing the judgment of an inferior court to the Court of Appeals ,
for reversal or modification , shall be by an appeal , which shall be
granted , as matter of right, to a party or privy against a party or
§ 734. (1.) Jurisdiction . As to jurisdiction of court of appeals , and superior
court , see acts regulating , pages 375 , 376 .
(2. ) Amount in controvery . The interest due upon a debt is a part of the
amount in controversy . Orth v. Clutz , 18 B. M. 223 ; Bracy v. Bracy , 12 Bush 153 ;
Bakewell v. Howell, 2 Met . 268 ; Fidler v . Hall , 2 Met . 461 .
(3. ) Where the matter in controversy exceeded fifty dollars (now one hundred dol-
lars ) the court of appeals had jurisdiction on an appeal by the plaintiffs from a
judgment in their favor for three dollars , but the defendant could not appeal as the
judgment against him was for less than fifty dollars (now one hundred dollars ).
Brandies v. Stewart , 1 Met. 395 ; Revill v. Pettit, 3 Met. 315 ; Tipton v. Chambers ,
1 Met . 565 .
(4. ) Where several are jointly suable on the same contract for separate amounts ,
the aggregate claimed against all determines the jurisdiction . Wilde v. Haycraft ,
2 Duv . 309 .
(5. ) Land. Where the title to land is put in issue either party may appeal with-
out reference to its value . Moore v. Boner , 7 Bush 26 ; Caskey v. Lewis , 15 B. M. 27 .
(6. ) Granting of by lower court. The lower court has no power to grant an
appeal after the expiration of the term at which the judgment was rendered , or the
motion for a new trial overruled . Wright v. Woolfolk , 14 Bush 308 ; City v. Elrod ,
14 Bush 216 ,

(7.) By Court of Appeals . Where the appeal granted by the lower court is not
taken, or where the record under the appeal in lower court is not filed in time , and
the appeal is dismissed , an appeal may be granted by the clerk of the Court of
Appeals within the time allowed . City v. Elrod , 14 Bush 216 ; Wearen v. Smith ,
80 Ky. 216.
(8.) Suing out a summons against the appellee is not made a requisite to the
granting of the appeal , and is not necessary to secure an appeal . The appeal is taken
by, and dates from , the filing of the record (now copy of the judgment ) with the clerk
of the Court of Appeals . Jones v. Finnell , 8 Bush 25.
(9.) Accepting satisfaction of judgment bars right to appeal . Where a
party recovers judgment upon an indivisible claim, and collects it by execution , he
can not afterward maintain an appeal on the ground that his judgment did not
amount to as much as he sued for; and if, pending the appeal , the appellant accepts
satisfactionof the judgment in his favor , his appeal will, on proper application , be
dismissed. Paine v . Woolley , 80 Ky . 568 ; Com . v. South , 80 Ky . 582. But see

It details how appeals are granted, the necessity of suing out a summons, the effect of accepting satisfaction of a judgment on the right to appeal, and the cessation of the right to appeal for fiduciaries. It also specifies who must be a party to an appeal and lists various orders and judgments from which an appeal may be taken, including those related to attachments, receivers, division of land, allotting dower, ad quod damnum proceedings, confirmation of sales, witness attendance fees, divorce allowances, disbarment of attorneys, removal of jailers, and tax collection.

or judgment which relates to an office, franchise or freehold , Smith v. Cochran , T
Bush 147 ; from a judgment on an invalid bail bond , Pauer v. Simon , 6 Bush 514 ; or
upon service in another county than that in which the action is pending , Ruby v.
Grace , 2 Duv. 540 ; or without service of process , Long v. Montgomery , 6 Bush 394.
(19.) From a decision of the circuit court reversing or affirming a decision of the
county court on an application for merchants license , Dougherty v. Com ., 14 B. М.
239 ; from an order adjudging priority between claimants and refusing to substitute
one party to the lien of another , Hearn v. Lander , 11 Bush 669 ; from an adverse
judgment of the circuit court on the claim of a county creditor who has appealed
from an order of the county court rejecting it, Washington county v. Thompson , 13
Bush 239. A nominated executor may appeal from an order of the circuit court
rejecting a will , Pryor v. Mizner, 79 Ky . 232 .
(20. ) Final order defined . See note 1 to sec . 368 ; and 8 Met . 309 ; 2 Bush 153 ;
2 Bush 580 .
(21. ) Orders not final. The quashal of a return upon a summons , Wearen v.
Smith , 80 Ky . 216 ; judgment against a party directing him to pay a certain amount
of money when further ordered , Tinly v. Martin, 80 Ky . 463 ; an order requiring the
plaintiff to elect which of two causes of action he will prosecute , Jones v. Johnson ,
10 Bush 649 ; order discharging an attachment on motion before final hearing , Leet
v. Lockett , 4 Met. 56 ; 4 Met. 108 ; dissolving injunction and continuing case , Rod-
man v. Forline , 2 Met. 325 ; order remanding a case to a county from which it had
been moved by change of venue , Turner v. Browder , 18 B. M. 825 ; an order made at
the same term setting aside an order dismissing a petition , Curd v. Dodds , 6 Bush 681 ;
can not be appealed from .
(22. ) Decisions upon writs of habeas corpus can not be appealed from , Weddington
v. Sloan , 15 B. M. 147 ; nor can an order punishing for contempt , Patton v. Harris , 15
B. M. 607 ; nor can an order of the county court refusing to qualify a person as deputy
sheriff , Applegate v. Applegate , 4 Met. 236 ; nor can an order requiring an executor to
give security ; until it is enforced , Atwell v. Helm , 7 Bush 504 ; nor can an order con-
firming biennial settlements made in the circuit court in an equitable action brought
in that court by a guardian to enable him to settle his accounts from time to time ,
Clark v. Anderson , 10 Bush 99 ; nor can an order appointing a commissioner to
ascertain facts in regard to alleged usury in a claim , Pryor v. Smith , 4 Bush 379 ; nor
can an order for a rule against a surety in a bond given to discharge an attachment to
show cause why he should not pay bond , Inman v. Stratton , 4 Bush 445 ; nor can an
order overruling a motion to set aside the verdict of a jury when there has been no
judgment on the verdict , Rucker v. Pritchett , 3 Bush 689 .

§ 737. ( 1. ) Transcript -duty of clerks . As to when clerk of inferior court
may be required to deliver transcript without pay , see note 2 to sec . 671 .
( 2. ) Where the clerk of the court of appeals has allowed a party , or his counsel ,
to use the original transcript in a case under an agreement to pay therefor , as if a
copy had been actually made, and the party thus using the record is successful , he is
entitled to have the cost of a copy taxed as a part of his cost against the unsuccessful
party. Parrish v. Ferguson , 88 Ky . 18.
( 3. ) Entire record , when only partial necessary . An appeal will not be dis-
missed because a party brings the entire record up in a proceeding under chapter 3,
title 10. If a party sees proper to bring the entire record he can do so, but he must
pay the entire costs, although the judgment be reversed . Murrell v. McAlister ,
78 Ky . 73 .
(4.) Schedule-notice-partial record-cross -appeal . So much of the code
as requires the judge to direct what part of the record is to be copied must be
regarded as repealed by the act repealing so much of the code as required an assign-
ment of errors . The appellant may still bring a part of the record by filing his
schedule within ninety days from the time he prays the appeal , and the filing of the
schedule with the clerk is all the notice required to be given to the appellee ; but he
will not be permitted to file a partial record unless he has filed his schedule within
ninety days , and this rule applies as well to appeals involving the settlement of
estates as in ordinary cases . When the entire record is filed in time no schedule is
necessary . If the appellee desires to prosecute a cross-appeal in this court he may
file his schedule in the court below , either before or after the schedule is filed by the
appellant . L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Brice , 83 Ky. 210 .
(5. ) What record should show . The appellant must exhibit in the transcript so
much of the record as will show affirmatively that the decision complained of is
erroneous . When the transcript shows that the record contained a paper which may
have contained matter that would sustain the decision the appellant must include
such paper , or this court will presume the decision appealed from to have been
correct . Huffaker v. Bank , 13 Bush 644 ; Bowman v. Holloway, 14 Bush 426.

§ 742. (1. ) Defects in record-how supplied . When the record is defective
by reason of a clerical misprision time will be given by the court to have it corrected
and brought up by certiorari . Williams v. Thompson , 80 Ky. 825 .
(2. ) But a rehearing will not be granted in order to enable the parties to perfect
the transcript . Yeager v. Groves , 78 Ky. 278 ; Christopher v. Searcy , 12 Bush 171 .
(3.) The court of appeals will, of their motion , award a certiorari to the clerk of
the inferior court where there is a strong presumption from the facts appearing in the
record that the clerk has copied a paper erroneously . Franklin v. Hall, 16 B. M. 472 .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Appeals to the Court of Appeals
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines procedures for appeals to the Court of Appeals. It details how writs of certiorari and subpoena duces tecum can be issued to obtain necessary parts of a record from inferior courts. It also specifies that appellants may be required to secure costs and sets a two-year limitation on appeals. § 749 [888 , 889 ] . 1. Who to take bond and issue supersedeas .
The bond must be executed before the clerk of the court rendering
the judgment , if the appeal be granted by that court . In other
cases , it must be executed before the clerk of the Court of Appeals .
2. The clerk of the court rendering the judgment shall issue the
supersedeas , if the bond be executed before him before the expi-
ration of the time for filing a copy of the record in the clerk's
office of the Court of Appeals pursuant to section 738. In other
cases , it must be issued by the clerk of the Court of Appeals .
§ 750 [890 ] . Supersedeas may be discharged for insufficiency
of bond . The appellee may move the Court of Appeals , if in
session ; or , on reasonable notice to the appellant, may move a judge
of said court in vacation , to discharge the supersedeas ; and, if the
court or judge shall consider the sureties insufficient , or the bond
substantially defective in securing the rights of the appellee , the court
or judge shall make an order discharging the supersedeas , unless a
good bond with sufficient sureties be forthwith executed . The order ,
if made by a judge , shall be in writing and signed by him ; upon the
filing of which , or of a certified copy of the order , if made by the
court , in the clerk's office of the inferior court , executions and other
proceedings for enforcing the judgment may be immediately taken ,
as if no supersedeas had been issued. But another supersedeas may
be issued by the clerk of the Court of Appeals upon the execution
of a new and lawful bond , with sufficient sureties, as hereinbefore
provided .
§ 751 [ 891 ] . Part of judgment may be superseded . The super-
sedeas may be issued to stay proceedings on a part of a judgment , and
§ 749. (1. ) Issuing of supersedeas . A supersedeas can not be issued by the
clerk of the court rendering the judgment unless an appeal was granted by that
court . Jones v. Green , 12 Bush 127.
(2. ) Where an appeal is prayed in the lower court , and a supersedeas bond exe-
cuted in that court and an order of supersedeas issued , if that appeal is abandoned ,
and an appeal granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals , the appellee is entitled,
upon motion made in the Court of Appeals , to have the supersedeas discharged with
damages . City v. Elrod , 14 Bush 216.

§ 755 [895] . 1. Manner in which cross -appeal taken . The
appellee , if he have filed an assignment of errors in the clerk's office
of the inferior court , pursuant to the provisions of sub -sections 4 and
7 of section 737 ; or, in any case, upon filing such assignment in the
Court of Appeals , may obtain a cross - appeal , at any time before trial ,
by an entry on the records of the Court of Appeals .
2. The failure of the appellee to take a cross - appeal after assign-
ing cross - errors pursuant to the provisions of sub-sections 4 or 7
of section 737 , and not otherwise shall bar his right to an appeal .
3. The failure of an appellant to prosecute an appeal; or his
dismissal of it after the appellee shall have assigned cross -errors
pursuant to the provisions of sub-sections 4 or 7 of section 737, or
sub -section 1 of this section , shall not prevent the appellee from
prosecuting a cross - appeal .
§ 756 [896 ] . 1. Assignment of errors-must be substantial to
authorize reversal . Every appellant or cross -appellant must , by
his assignment of errors , specify the particular errors on which he
§ 755. (1. ) Cross-appeal . If a cross-appeal is desired the appellee may file his
schedule in the court below , either before or after the schedule is filed by the appel-
lant . No schedule is required when the entire record is copied . L. & N. R. R. Co.
v. Brice , 83 Ky . 210 .
(2.) A cross -appeal can not be considered when the parties to be affected by it are
not before the court . Daviess Co. v. Howard , 13 Bush 101.
(3. ) " The legal effect of the affirmance on the original appeal , there being a
reversal of the judgment on the cross -appeal , was not to leave the judgment unre-
versed , but to decide that there was no error to the prejudice of the appellant , and
that he was not entitled to a reversal . " In such case the appellee is not entitled to
damages on the bond executed by the appellant . Wade v. Bank , 11 Bush 697 .
(4. ) Cross -appeal is only allowable in behalf of the appellee against the appel-
lant, and not against a co-appellee . Smith v. Bank , 1 Met. 575 ; Gaar v. Banking
Co. , 11 Bush 180 .
§ 756. (1. ) Error must be substantial . To authorize a reversal the error
complained of must affect the substantial rights of the appellant , and that it does so
affect them must as clearly appear as the error itself. Williams v. Rogers , 14 Bush
776. And the court of appeals will presume that the decision of the lower court is
correct , unless it affirmatively appears from the record that the decision complained
of is erroneous . Huffaker v. Bank , 13 Bush 644 ; Bowman v. Holloway, 14 Bush 426 .
(2.) Change in law. A judgment which was right when rendered will not
become erroneous by a subsequent change in the law, and will not for such reason be
reversed . Parrish v. Hill, 2 Duv. 396 .
(3.) Judgment correct , but reason wrong . A correct judgment must be
affirmed , although it may have been erroneously made , to rest on a different ground
by the lower court , Poor v. Robinson , 13 Bush 290 ; or given for a wrong or
insufficient reason . Ireland v. Berryman , 3 Bush 356 .

The text includes several case examples illustrating what constitutes a substantial error and what does not, focusing on procedural fairness and the impact of errors on the outcome of the case.

340 [TITLE XVIII .APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS .
Trial and Decision .
ture combined, to amend an assignment of errors or cross -errors ,
after reasonable notice to the adverse party .
Amendment of May 5, 1880 : §1 . That section 756 of the Code
of Practice in civil cases be amended so as to read as follows :
1. Every appellant or cross - appellant must, by his assignment of
errors , specify the particular errors on which he means to rely ;
and no others shall be passed upon by the court , nor shall a judg-
ment be reversed or modified , except for an error to the preju-
dice of the substantial rights of the party complaining thereof;
but the Court of Appeals may, on motion , after ten days' notice
to the adverse party , or his attorney , allow a party to file a tran-
script of an additional portion of the record . (Assignment of
errors not required , see amendment to see . 737.)
§ 757 [897] . Motion by appellee to dismiss appeal-when
heard . If it appear from the record that an appeal was improperly
granted , or that the appellant's right to prosecute it further has
ceased , the appellee may, upon stating the grounds in writing , move
the court to dismiss the appeal . The motion shall not be heard ,
without the appellant's consent , before the day on which the case is
set for trial on the docket .
Amendment of May 5 , 1880 : §1 . That section 757 of an act
regulating practice in civil cases be amended so as to read as fol-
lows : If it appear from the record that an appeal was improperly
granted , or that the appellant's right to prosecute it further has
§ 757. ( 1.) Right to appeal -want of, how shown . When an appeal is
sought to be dismissed on the ground that it is barred by limitation , a written plea
must be filed . The objection can not be made by motion . Riley v. Reed , 13 Bush 411 .
(2.) A motion to dismiss an appeal on the ground that appellant had compromised
with appellee will not be entertained unless the plea is presented by a verified answer ,
as required in sec . 758. Empire Co. v. McIntosh , 82 Ky . 554 .
(3. ) The fact that appellant had agreed to pay, and appellée to receive , a less sum
than the judgment as a compromise in full satisfaction of the judgment appealed
from can not be pleaded in the Court of Appeals to show that appellant had no right
to prosecute the appeal . Parks v. Doty, 13 Bush 727 .
(4. ) Judgment-accepting satisfaction of. See notes 9, 10, 11 to sec. 734 .
(5.) Fiduciary. Whenever any person occupying a fiducial relation resigns or
is removed from office his right to prosecute an appeal terminates , and this fact may
be brought to the notice of the court by an answer ; so, when the right of a bankrupt
passes to his assignee . Parks v. Doty , 13 Bush 727 .
(6.) Death of appellant . When appellant dies after judgment and before appeal ,
or pending the appeal , his death should be taken advantage of in the Court ofAppeals
by a plea under the provisions of sec. 758. When an appeal is prosecuted in the
name of a dead person the judgment reversing the case is not void. Spalding v.
Wathen , 7 Bush 659 .

It outlines requirements for assignments of error, grounds for dismissing an appeal (such as improper granting or cessation of the appellant's right to prosecute), and how to present objections like bar by limitation, compromise, or the death of an appellant. It also specifies that certain rights to appeal terminate upon resignation or removal from a fiduciary office, or when a bankrupt's rights pass to an assignee.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice from 1890 outlines procedures for appeals to the Court of Appeals. It details how an appellee can move to dismiss an appeal, the conditions under which such a motion can be heard, and the process for affirming a judgment as a delay case if the appeal is deemed to be for delay only. It also includes an amendment from March 24, 1888, allowing a party to appeal a judgment for only part of the demand or property sued for. Additionally, it touches upon the court's power to make rules, issue mandates, grant rehearings, and handle defective records or contempt.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890, Section 763
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the conditions under which the Court of Appeals will not reverse or modify a judgment from an inferior court. It specifies that judgments against defendants only constructively summoned and who haven't appeared, or judgments that can be set aside or modified by the rendering court after the term, require a motion to be made and overruled in the inferior court before an appeal can be heard. It also details the court's power to correct clerical errors and enforce its mandates.

§ 764 [904. ] Damages upon affirmance. Upon the affirmance
of, or the dismissal of an appeal from , ajudgment for the payment of
money , the collection of which , in whole or part , has been super-
seded , as provided in chapter 2 of this title , ten per cent . damages on

Amendment of April 19, 1888 : §1 . That section 764 of the
Civil Code of Practice be , and the same is, amended so as to add
to said section, at the end thereof , the language following , to-
wit : And upon the affirmance of, or the dismissal of an appeal
from , a judgment for money rendered against any insurance ,
railroad , corporation or company , or against any corporation not
created by or organized under the laws of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , ten per centum damages on the amount of the judg-
ment appealed from shall be awarded against the appellant ,
although such judgment be not superseded .
§ 765 [ 905 ] . Written opinions to be delivered . The court must
deliver written opinions in all cases .
§ 766 [906] . Duty of reporter. The reporter of the decisions
of the Court of Appeals shall make a brief statement of the facts
involved in the decisions, unless they be stated in the opinion of the
court , and a brief statement of the legal propositions of counsel on
both sides , and a statement of the authorities cited by counsel in sup-
port of each proposition .
§ 767 [907 ] . Provisions concerning revivor applicable. The

§ 764. (1.) Damages on affirmance . See notes 1 and 2 to sec . 748 , as to
necessity for an order of supersedeas issuing .
(2.) The appellee is not entitled to damages against the appellant when the judg-
ment of the lower court is affirmed on the original and reversed on the cross appeal .
Wade v. Bank , 11 Bush 697 .
(3. ) The claim for damages does not constitute a lien upon the property of the
appellant . It is secured if at all by the sureties in the supersedeas bond . N. & C.
Bridge Co. v . Douglas , 12 Bush 673 .

classification.title: Damages upon affirmance.
classification.summary: This statute outlines damages awarded upon the affirmance or dismissal of an appeal from a judgment for the payment of money. It specifies that ten percent damages will be awarded against the appellant if the collection of the judgment was superseded. An amendment from April 19, 1888, adds that ten percent damages will be awarded against the appellant for judgments against insurance, railroad corporations, or companies not created under Kentucky law, even if the judgment is not superseded.

There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The amendment, while broadening the scope of damages to certain types of corporations, does not introduce any racial element. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAP. I.] LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT. 345

Jurisdiction.

CHAPTER 5. *Clerk.*
6. *Marshall.*
7. *Practice and general provisions.*

CHAPTER I.

*Jurisdiction.*

§ 768. Jurisdiction of generally.
§ 769. Jurisdiction in attachment cases.
§ 770. Power of as to rules of court.
§ 771. To be always open.
§ 772. To have control over its judgments for sixty days.
§ 773. May provide for payment of taxes in ordering sales.

§ 768 [775]. **Jurisdiction of generally.** The Louisville chancery court shall exist as heretofore, and shall have such equity jurisdiction in Jefferson county as the circuit courts have in their respective counties, with like powers in this regard, and subject to the same regulations, except as is prescribed by the provisions of this title.

§ 769. **Jurisdiction in attachment cases.** It shall also have concurrent jurisdiction with the Jefferson court of common pleas of all actions wherein an attachment is issued.

§ 770 [821]. **Power of, as to rules of court.** It shall have power to prescribe such rules for the transaction of its business, and the government of its officers, as are not inconsistent with law.

§ 771 [777]. **To be always open.** It shall have no stated terms, but shall be deemed to be always open for the transaction of business.

§ 772 [800]. **To have control over its judgments for sixty days.** It shall have such control over its judgments, for sixty days, as circuit courts have over their judgments during the term in which they are rendered.

§ 773. **May provide for payment of taxes in ordering sales.** It may, in actions for the sale of real property, determine summarily, with or without written pleadings, the amount of any State or municipal tax or assessment upon the property to be sold; and shall provide for the payment of the same in the judgment; and, if the plaintiff fail to ask therefor, the purchaser shall be entitled at any time before payment of the purchase price to a credit for the amount thereof.

346 [TITLE XIX .LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Chancellor and Vice-Chancellor .
CHAPTER II .
Chancellor and Vice- Chancellor .
2774. Election and term of office of chancellor .
775. Election and term of office of vice -chancellor .
776. Compensation of chancellor and vice -chancellor .
2777. Powers and duties of chancellor and vice -chancellor .
778. When and how special chancellor may be elected .
§ 774. Election and term of office of chancellor . The office
of chancellor is continued as heretofore , and the present incumbent
shall hold his office for the term for which he was elected . There-
after, a chancellor of said court shall be elected by the qualified
voters within its jurisdiction , when the judge of the Jefferson circuit
court shall be elected ; and vacancies in the office of chancellor shall
be filled in like manner as vacancies in the office of judge of the
Jefferson circuit court .
§ 775. Election and term of office of vice -chancellor . The
office of vice -chancellor shall continue as heretofore . The present
incumbent shall hold his office for the term for which he was elected .
A vice -chancellor of said court shall be elected by the qualified
voters within its jurisdiction on the first Monday in August , 1878 ,
and every six years thereafter ; and shall have the same qualifications
as the chancellor of said court , and hold his office for the term of
six years .
§ 776. Compensation of chancellor and vice -chancellor . The
compensation of the chancellor and vice -chancellor shall remain and
be paid as is now provided by law .
§ 777. Powers and duties of chancellor and vice -chancellor .
The chancellor shall be the presiding judge of said court ; and, dur-
ing his absence or incapacity to sit, his duties shall be discharged by
the vice - chancellor . It shall also be the duty of the vice -chancellor
to hear and determine any causes or questions which may be assigned
to him by the chancellor . But it shall not be necessary for the
record to show that the vice-chancellor has jurisdiction, and neither
of said judges shall vacate or modify any judgment rendered by the
other . The vice - chancellor shall also have power to grant injunctions ,
restraining orders , and attachments .
§ 778. When and how special chancellor may be elected. If
the chancellor and vice -chancellor be absent , or can not properly pre-
side , a special chancellor may be elected , as is provided in case of the
absence of a circuit judge or of his incapacity to sit .

CHAP . II .] 347LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Chancellor and Vice-Chancellor .
Act of April 13, 1880 , to amend an act entitled " an act to pro-
vide for the election and compensation of a vice- chancellor of the
Louisville chancery court , and define his duties in connection with
said court and the Jefferson court of common pleas , and to pro-
vide a room and offices for his court " approved March 26 , 1872 :
§ 1. That it shall be the duty of the clerk of the Louisville
chancery court to indorse " Vice-chancellor's Court " upon the
petition in every third action commenced in said Louisville
chancery court at the time of and in the order in which said
petitions are filed ; and all the steps thereafter taken in said
actions shall be taken before the vice-chancellor ; and it shall be
the duty of the clerk of the Jefferson court of common pleas to
indorse " Vice -chancellor's Court " upon the petition or tran-
script in every third action commenced in, or appealed to, said
Jefferson court of common pleas , at the time of and in the order
in which said petitions or transcripts are respectively filed in his
office ; and all the steps thereafter taken in said actions shall be
taken before the vice-chancellor .
§ 2. Said vice- chancellor shall have, as to the actions and pro-
ceedings which shall come before him under the provisions of
this act, the same civil , common law , and equity jurisdiction ,
original and appellate, and like power and authority over the
same , as by existing laws appertain to the Jefferson court of
common pleas and the Louisville chancery court .
§3 . The laws now in force and applicable to the Jefferson
court of common pleas in regard to juries and the summoning
and selection of the same , and the trial of actions by jury , shall
apply to and govern said vice- chancellor's court .
§ 4. It shall be the duty of the vice - chancellor to discharge the
duties of the chancellor , or of the judge of the Jefferson court
of common pleas , in the absence of either said judge or said
chancellor , or in case of the incapacity of either of them, from
any reason , to sit in any action in their respective courts ; and it
shall be the duty of the chancellor to discharge the duties of the
vice -chancellor in equitable actions , and the duty of the judge of
the Jefferson court of common pleas to discharge the duties
of said vice-chancellor in ordinary actions pending before the
vice-chancellor , in the absence of the vice - chancellor , or in case
of his incapacity, from any reason , to
sit
inany such equitable
or ordinary actions

§ 8. The vice-chancellor shall have the same power to assign
cases to the circuit court for the ninth judicial district as the
judge of the Jefferson court of common pleas now has under
existing laws : Provided , The judge of said circuit court assents
to such assignment ; but it shall not be necessary , in any case ,
for the record to show such assent .
§ 9. Whenever the said court of common pleas shall fall so far
behind its trial docket as, in the opinion of the judge thereof, it
shall be deemed expedient and necessary , the vice -chancellor
shall have the power to call said docket , and try causes thereon ,
se long as said court of common pleas shall continue behind its
said docket ; and whenever said vice - chancellor shall fall so far
behind his trial docket for ordinary actions as, in his opinion , it
shall be deemed expedient and necessary , the judge of the Jef-
ferson court of common pleas shall have the power to call said
docket , and try causes thereon , so long as said vice chancellor
shall continue behind his said docket .
Act of April 4, 1884, to change the name of the vice - chancellor's
court at Louisville to the Louisville law and equity court , and to
regulate proceedings therein : §1 . The vice -chancellor's court
held in the city of Louisville shall hereafter be called and known
as the Louisville law and equity court , and the vice - chancellor of
said vice - chancellor's court shall be called and known as the
judge of said Louisville law and equity court . And wherever
in any act or law the words vice-chancellor's court appear they
shall be read as Louisville law and equity court , and wherever in
any act or law the words vice - chancellor , or vice - chancellor of the
Louisville chancery court shall appear, they shall be read as
judge of the Louisville law and equity court .
