§ 779 [804 , 806] . To be appointed by chancellor-powers and
duties of. The chancellor shall appoint a commissioner , who shall
hold his office during four years , unless removed , as he may be , at the
pleasure of the chancellor ; and the commissioner may , with the
approval of the chancellor , appoint deputies. He shall discharge the
duties , and have the powers appertaining to the office , of a master in
chancery , and as provided by law and the rules of the court .
§ 780 [805 , 806 ] . Commissioner and deputies to take oath .
Before entering upon the discharge of his duties, the commissioner
shall appear in open court , and take an oath that he will honestly ,
impartially , and to the best of his judgment , discharge all the duties
of his office , without favor or respect of persons , and render true and
just reports of all his actings and doings as such ; and each of his
deputies shall take such oath before entering upon the discharge of
his duties .
§ 781. May make conveyances of real property-number of
deeds . Conveyances of real property , which are authorized by law
to be made by a commissioner appointed by the court , may be made
by the commissioner . In cases of the partition of land , there shall be
but one deed , unless otherwise ordered by the court .

CHAP . IV.] 353LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Receiver .
§ 784. Appointment of and term of office . The chancellor
shall appoint receiver, who shall hold his office during four years ,
unless removed , as he may be, at the pleasure of the chancellor .
§ 785. Duties of with reference to moneys received-payment
of costs . It shall be the duty of the receiver to receive and pay ,
under order of court , all moneys paid into court , unless immediately
withdrawn. He shall deposit all funds received by him in a bank
organized under a charter granted by this Commonwealth , or by the
United States , and having a capital of not less than two hundred
thousand dollars , actually paid in , and which shall have given bond ,
with good sureties, approved by the court , in the penalty of one hun-
dred and fifty thousand dollars , payable to the clerk of the court , and
conditioned to pay all moneys deposited by the receiver, or his suc-
cessor, upon any check drawn by him under order of the court . An
account shall be kept by the clerk of all moneys paid into the court ,
and immediately withdrawn by the party entitled thereto ; and an
account of all moneys withdrawn by the receiver .

354 [TITLE XIX .LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Receiver .
court , conditioned that he will discharge his duties required in this
section . The court shall designate a place of deposit for such arti-
cles as may be committed to the charge of the receiver under this
section .
§ 788. Remedies against for breach of bond. Any person
interested may sue on any of the bonds mentioned in sections 785 ,
786 , and 787 , for a breach thereof ; and the court may , by rule and
attachment against the obligors therein , enforce their performance .
§ 789. When and how he shall report funds-advertisement
concerning . The receiver shall , on the first Friday in January and
July in each year , report to the court the aggregate amount of funds
in court , with the style of each cause in which there may be money ,
and the amount thereof ; and what sums have been ordered to be paid
which remain unpaid , and the names of the persons entitled thereto .
And on the first day of August in each year , he shall cause to be
published , in the daily paper in the city of Louisville having the
largest bona fide circulation , a statement of such sums as have
remained in court for more than two years , in what causes said sums
remain respectively , and how long they have remained ; and, if
ordered out , the names of the persons respectively entitled thereto-
for which , and to pay the expense of the publication , the receiver
shall be entitled to the sum of ten cents for each publication of each
and every sum and name so published , to be deducted from the said
respective sums .
§ 790. When money to be paid into State Treasury . One
year after each publication provided for in section 789 , the court shall
order the receiver to pay into the Treasury of the Commonwealth
all sums which shall have remained in court during ten years prior to
said publication ; and the receiver shall pay such sums , and furnish
to the Auditor of the State a statement thereof ; and the treasurer
of the State shall execute duplicate receipts therefor , one of which
shall be filed and preserved in the Auditor's office , and the other be
kept by the receiver . The money so paid into the Treasury shall be
accounted for as a part of the ordinary revenues of the Common-
wealth ; but , whenever the person entitled thereto , or his personal
representative , shall apply to the court and make due proof of the
justice of his claim , the court shall certify it to the Auditor , who ,
upon said certificate, shall draw his warrant on the Treasury , in favor
of the said person , for the said sum without interest .

classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the duties, election, term of office, and qualifications of the Marshal of the Louisville Chancery Court. It details responsibilities related to process execution, sales, fees, and the appointment of deputies. The text also references the clerk's duties and fees.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the administrative and procedural aspects of the Marshal's office within the Louisville Chancery Court. It focuses on the election, duties, and compensation of this official. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Louisville Chancery Court Marshal Duties and Powers
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, powers, and fees of the Marshal of the Louisville Chancery Court. It details their responsibilities in attending court, executing orders and precepts, making sales, and the conditions under which process may be executed by the sheriff or coroner if the marshal is interested. It also specifies the fees the marshal is entitled to for various services.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative and procedural functions of the Marshal of the Louisville Chancery Court. It details their appointment, oath, bond, duties, powers, and fees. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
358 [TITLE XIX .LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Marshal.
Act of March 3, 1888 : §1 . That for every lot or parcel of
property offered or cried for sale by the marshal of the Louis-
ville chancery court under any order or judgment of court he
shall receive five per cent. on the first three hundred dollars and
one per cent. on all sums over that amount, and he shall receive
his fees and costs , including the advertisement and care of
property in his custody and keeping , and for all other services
he shall receive the same fees as are allowed to sheriffs for similar
services , all of which shall be taxed as other costs in the case :
Provided , The sale is not set aside on account of some act or
omission of the marshal : Provided , That his compensation for
one sale shall not exceed one thousand dollars .
§ 2. All laws and parts of laws in conflict with this act are
hereby repealed .
§ 805. Not required to leave county to execute process . The
marshal shall not be obliged to travel out of the county of Jeffer-
son to execute any process , nor shall he be entitled to any mileage
therefor . Process of the court to other counties may be issued and
executed and returned in the same manner and by the same officer as
process from a circuit court .
§ 806. What process and orders from Court of Appeals
directed to . Process and orders from the Court of Appeals , in causes
appealed from the Louisville chancery court, and required to be exe-
cuted in Jefferson county , shall be directed as in case of an action or
proceeding in said chancery court .
Act of March 20 , 1880 , to legalize the administration of oaths to
appraisers of property by the marshal of the Louisville chancery
court and his deputies : § 1. That the acts of the several marshals
of the Louisville chancery court and their several deputies , in
administering oaths to appraisers of property in cases of judicial
sales , be , and the same are, declared legal and valid , and as
effective as though power had, heretofore and prior to such
administration of oaths, been specially conferred by law upon
such marshals and their deputies .
§ 2. That the marshal of the Louisville chancery court and his
deputies shall hereafter have power to administer oaths to
appraisers of property in cases of judicial sales , to the same
extent as , and similar in all respects to, the power now by law
conferred upon sheriffs in such cases .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Louisville Chancery Court Practice and General Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines practice and general provisions for the Louisville Chancery Court. It details when defendants must answer summons, when defenses must be filed, the timing of subsequent pleadings, and procedures for filing and effect of pleadings. It also covers interrogatories, depositions, trial dockets, new trials, vacating judgments, revivors, execution issuance, petition amendments, judicial sales, bail objections, indemnity bonds, receiver's money, actions on officer bonds, and appeals. Specifically, it states that the time for a defendant to answer a summons is twenty days if served in Jefferson county and thirty days if served elsewhere in the state.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural rules for the Louisville Chancery Court, including timelines for filing legal documents, taking depositions, and appealing judgments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The rules appear to be general procedural guidelines applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

360 [TITLE XIX.LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Practice and General Provisions .
such service elsewhere in the State ; or , if the defendant be construct-
ively summoned , the defense shall be filed in sixty days thereafter .
§ 810 [783 ] . Time of filing pleadings subsequent to answer .
Every pleading subsequent to the answer shall be filed in fourteen
days after the pleading is filed to which it responds , but the court
may extend the time for pleading .
§ 811 [784 ] . Pleadings may be filed in clerk's office effect of.
The filing of a pleading in the clerk's office within the proper time ,
and causing it to be noted upon the clerk's memorandum-book and
rule docket , shall be equivalent to a filing in court .
§ 812 [785] . When interrogatories must be answered . Inter-
rogatories annexed to a pleading shall be answered when the respon-
sive pleading is required to be filed .
§ 813 [808] . Unless by order of court depositions to be taken
upon interrogatories . All depositions shall be taken upon inter-
rogatories , unless the court shall otherwise provide by rule , or by an
order in the cause .
§ 814 [809 ] . How and before whom depositions taken and
certified . Depositions shall be taken and certified in the manner ,
and by the officers , directed in this Code , subject to the following
modifications :
1. Depositions in the city of Louisville shall be taken before
the commissioner of the court , and no commission shall be
required therefor .
2. Notice to the adverse party shall not be necessary ; but the
court shall , by rule , prescribe days for the filing of interrogato-
ries , and the time they shall remain in the clerk's office .
3. If a deposition be taken upon interrogatories , neither party
nor his agent or attorney shall be present at the examination of
the witness , unless he have notified the adverse party of his inten-
tion to be present ; and , in such case , the party in whose behalf
the deposition is to be taken shall notify the adverse party of the
time and place of the taking .
4. When it appears by the certificate of the officer that one
only of the parties was present at the examination of the wit-
ness , the reason for permitting him to be present shall be stated ;
and when a notice to attend has been given , the evidence thereof
shall be annexed to the certificate.

362 [TITLE XIX .LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
Practice and General Provisions .
§ 822 [802] . Party may show cause against revivor thirty
days after notice . Within thirty days after the service of an order
to revive an action , as is provided , in section 502 , the party upon
whom it is made may show cause against the revivor ; and, if suffi-
cient cause be not shown within that time, the action shall stand
revived .
§ 823 [803 ] . Warning order to allow sixty days to show cause
against revivor . An order warning parties to appear and show
cause why an action should not be revived , in the cases mentioned
in section 504 , shall allow sixty days for their appearance ; and, if
within the said period , sufficient cause be not shown to the contrary ,
the action shall stand revived .
§ 824 [798 ] . Execution not to issue until fifteen days after
judgment . No execution shall be had of any judgment within fif-
teen days after its rendition , unless otherwise directed by the court .
§ 825. Plaintiff may amend petition before answer . The
plaintiff may , without leave, amend his petition at any time before
answer is filed .
§ 826. Purchasers at judicial sales to make deposit . The
court may , by rules, require purchasers at judicial sales to deposit
with the marshal , at the time of sale , a specified sum of money, suffi-
cient to cover the expenses of a resale ; and, if such deposit be not
made at the time, the marshal shall at once resell the property .
§ 827 [ 810 ] . Terms of sales of property . Sales of property
shall be for cash or on reasonable credit , or for part cash and part on
reasonable credit , as the court may direct ; but a sale of personal
property shall not be on a longer credit than four months .

§ 2. When the judge of the Jefferson court of common pleas
can not properly preside in an action or special proceeding pend-
ing before him , such action or special proceeding shall be trans-
ferred by the clerk of said court or the judge thereof to the vice-
chancellor of the Louisville chancery court , and all further steps
and proceedings in such action or special proceeding shall be had
before said vice -chancellor ; and if, for any cause , the vice - chan-
cellor can not properly preside in such action or special proceed-
ing , the same shall be transferred to the Jefferson circuit court
and there tried .
§3 . When the chancellor of the Louisville chancery court
can not properly preside in an action or special proceeding pend-
ing before him , such action or special proceedings shall be trans-
ferred to the vice -chancellor of the Louisville chancery court ,
and all further steps and proceedings in such action or special
proceeding shall be had before said vice-chancellor ; and if, for
any cause , the vice-chancellor can not properly preside in an
action or special proceeding so transferred , a special judge may
be chosen to try the same as special judges for circuit courts
are chosen .
§4 . When the vice - chancellor of the Louisville chancery
court can not properly preside in an ordinary action or special
proceeding at law pending before him , such action or special
proceeding shall be transferred to the judge of the Jefferson
court of common pleas, and all further steps and proceeding in
such action or special proceeding shall be had before the judge
of the Jefferson court of common pleas ; and if, for any cause ,
the judge of the Jefferson court of common pleas can not
properly preside in an action or special proceeding so trans-
ferred , the same shall be tranferred to the Jefferson circuit court .
§5 . When the vice-chancellor of the Louisville chancery
court can not properly preside in an equitable action or special
proceeding in equity pending before him, such action or special
proceeding shall be transferred to the chancellor of the Louis-
ville chancery court , and all further steps and proceedings in
such action or special proceeding shall be had before the chan--
cellor; and if, for any cause , the chancellor can not properly
preside in any action or special proceeding so transferred , a
special judge may be chosen to try the same as special judges for
circuit courts are chosen .

366 [TITLE XX .JEFFERSON COURT OF COMMON PLEAS .
Concluding Provisions .
§ 6. Any action or special proceeding pending before the
judge of the Jefferson court of common pleas, or the chancellor
of the Louisville chancery court , or the vice -chancellor of the
Louisville chancery court , in which any party shall file with the
clerk of the court his affidavit that the judge before whom the
same is pending will not afford him a fair and impartial trial ,
shall , upon the filing of such affidavit , be deemed a case in which
such judge can not properly preside .
§ 7. The provisions of sections 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 of this act for
transferring cases shall apply also when the presiding judge of
the court from which the transfer is to be made is a special judge
chosen under the first section of this act .
§ 8. No special judge shall be chosen to preside in any action
or special proceeding pending before the judge of the Jefferson
court of common pleas , or the chancellor of the Louisville chan-
cery court , or the vice -chancellor of the Louisville chancery
court , except as provided in this act.
§ 9. The mode of choosing special judges under this act , and
the powers , responsibilities , and compensation for such special
judges , shall be the same as is provided by law for special judges
of circuit courts .
§ 10. Nothing in this act shall in any way affect the powers ,
duties , responsibilities , or compensations of special judges chosen
before this act takes effect .
§ 11. Whenever an ordinary action or proceeding pending
before the vice- chancellor is transferred to the equity docket , it
shall go to the vice -chancellor's equity docket . Whenever an
equitable action or proceeding pending before the vice - chancellor
is transferred to the common law docket , it shall go to the vice-
chancellor's common law docket . Like transfers from the
Jefferson court of common pleas shall be made to the Louisville
chancery court , and from the Louisville chancery court to the
Jefferson court of common pleas. The cases transferred under
this section shall not be counted in the allotment or distribution
of cases , as now provided by law .
CONCLUDING PROVISIONS .

838. Code regulates proceedings in cases hereafter commenced .
839. Time of going into effect .

368 APPENDIX .
Rent-Mode and Manner of Recovery .
18. How property distrained for rent disposed of.
19. Remedy against life tenant .
20. How rents dependent upon the life of another collected .
21. Remedies of husband after death of wife .
22. Remedies of personal representative .
23. Sale of property under distress warrant .
24. Rent may be replevied .
25. Indorsement on executions .
26. Damages for wrongfully distraining or attaching .
§ 1. Remedies for recovery . Rent may be recovered by distress
or action .
§ 2. Recovery for use and occupation-on verbal contract .
Unless the contract be in writing , a landlord may , by action , recover
reasonable satisfaction for the use and occupation of his land . If, on
the trial, a verbal contract be proven , whereby a rent certain in
amount was reserved , the plaintiff may nevertheless recover ; the
verbal contract being regarded as evidence of the amount recoverable .
§ 3. Rent to bear interest when due. Rent shall bear six per
cent. per annum interest from the time it is due .
§ 4. Distress warrant-when and how obtained. No land-
lord shall issue his own distress warrant . When rent is reserved in
§ 1. ( 1.) Joint tenant . One tenant in common or joint tenant has no lien upon
the interest of his co- tenant for the payment of rents collected by the latter before
partition . The claim is only a personal charge against him . Burch v. Burch , 82
Ky. 622.
(2. ) Rent contract not assignable . A contract to pay a certain amount as rent
and to make certain improvements is not assignable so as to vest the assignee with
the right to sue in his own name alone . Hicks v. Doty, 4 Bush 420 ; Helburn v.
Mofford , 7 Bush 169 .
§ 2. Use and occupation . An action for use and occupation of land can not
be maintained where the relation of landlord and tenant has never existed. R. & L.
Co. v . Rogers , 7 Bush 532 ; Hall v. Jacobs , 7 Bush 595 .
§ 4. ( 1.) Distress-when not authorized . The remedy by distress is only
available where the rent sought to be recovered is due and reserved in money ; and is
not authorized when the amount of rent is not ascertained , as where it is left to
arbitrators to fix. Myers v. Mayfield , 7 Bush 212 ; Skidmore v. Ensign, 6 J. J. M. 577 ;
Roberts v . Tennell , 4 J. J. M. 160 .
(2.) When warrant may issue. A distress warrant can not issue or be levied
until the day after the rent falls due . Fry v. Breckinridge , 7 B. M. 31 ; Gano v.
Hart , Hardin 304 ; nor can the distress be made in the night. 7 B. M. 31 .
( 3. ) A distress warrant is not void because it fails to state when and for what year's
rent the amount claimed is due . Mitchell v. Franklin , 8 J. J. M. 477 .
(4. ) Rent due on parol contract . Lessor by parol may distrain for rent against
his lessee , although the contract of renting is within the statute of frauds . Gudgell
v. Duvall , 4 J. J. M. 229 ; Roberts v. Tennell, 3 Mon. 247 .

classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal procedures and remedies for the recovery of rent. It covers various aspects such as how property distrained for rent is disposed of, remedies against life tenants, collection of rents dependent on another's life, remedies for a husband after his wife's death, remedies for personal representatives, sale of property under a distress warrant, rent replevin, indorsement on executions, and damages for wrongful distraining or attaching. It also details how rent can be recovered by distress or action, recovery for use and occupation on verbal contracts, and that rent bears interest from the time it is due. Specific subsections clarify when a distress warrant can be obtained and levied, and that distress cannot be made at night. It also notes that a distress warrant is not void for failing to state the specific year for which rent is claimed, and that a lessor by parol may distrain for rent.

(5.) Form of warrant . An officer is justified in levying a warrant , although it
does not state that the justice who issued it resides in the county where the land lies.
Asbell v. Tipton , 1 B. M. 300 .
(6.) Death of tenant . Where the tenant dies the landlord can not distrain unless
there is a sub-tenant until administration is granted . Hughes v. Sebre , 2 Mar. 227 .
(7.) Distress and action at same time . The landlord may sue for rent, and at
the same time distrain for it. Brandt v. Hyatt, 7 Bush 363 .
(8.) Property exempt-contract . Although by the terms of the lease the crop
to be grown on the rented premises was pledged for the payment of the rent , the
landlord's claim can not avail against the exemption allowed by statute to the widow
and children of the deceased tenant . Vinson v. Hallowell , 10 Bush 538 .
§5. ( 1.) Affidavit for attachment . " Before an attachment can be sued out
by the landlord against his tenant , he must make affidavit that there are reasonable
grounds for belief and that he believes unless an attachment be issued he will lose
hisrent ." Bishop v. McQuerry, 13 Bush 417. As to how an affidavit is made, see secs .
544, 551, Civil Code .
(2.) Bond . If the affidavit is not made and the bond executed before the attach-
ment is issued, the attachment will be quashed on motion . Brandt v. Hyatt, 7
Bush 363.
(3.) Grounds of attachment . Landlords suing out attachments are not to be
held to the same strictness of proof as parties proceeding under the Code of Practice
who attach to secure the payment of ordinary debts . The sale or removal from the
leased premises of any property bound by the landlord's lien is a violation of the
landlord's legal rights, and whenever so much of it is removed , or is about to be
removed, as to give the landlord reasonable ground to believe that the collection of
his rent will be endangered , his right to attach accrues . He is not bound to wait

the peace , police judge , or ajudge of a county court of the county
in which the tenement lies, make affidavit that there are reasonable
grounds for belief , and that he believes, unless an attachment be
issued, he will lose his rent ; whereupon , such officer shall issue an
attachment for the rent against the personal property of the person
liable for the same to any county the person suing out the same may
desire . But no such attachment shall issue until the plaintiff has
given bond , with good surety , to indemnify the defendant , if it
appear that the attachment has been wrongfully obtained . (Form
of affidavit and bond pages 413 , 414.)
§ 6. Court to which attachment returned-proceedings on-
privileges of tenant . All attachments issued under this chapter ,
for a sum of fifty dollars or less , exclusive of interest and costs ,
shall be returned before the officer issuing the attachment ; and
all those for a sum exceeding fifty dollars , and not exceeding one
hundred , shall be returned before the quarterly court of the county
whence it issued ; if over one hundred dollars , to the circuit court ;
and in either case the proceedings thereon shall be the same as on
other attachments according to the Civil Code ; and the defendant
shall also have the privilege of denying the tenancy , or his liability
to pay rent , as stated in the affidavit ; and, moreover , may repossess
himself of the property by executing bond in a manner similar to
that prescribed in the Civil Code of Practice , subject to similar pro-
ceedings , if forfeited , as is prescribed by the Code on such a bond .
( Form of attachment and bond pages 412 , 413.)
§ 7. Alienee or assignee may recover . If the owner or holder
alien or assign his estate or term , or the rent thereafter to fall due
thereon , his alienee or assignee may recover such rent .
§ 6. (1.) Papers -return of. It is not made the duty of the justice issuing the
attachment to deliver the affidavit and bond to the sheriff , nor to return them to the
clerk's office without notification or rule, and the failure to so return them is not
sufficient to repel the legal presumption that the justice discharged his duty in com-
plying with the requirements of the law before issuing the attachment . Brandt v.
Hyatt, 7 Bush 363 .
(2.) Trial in circuit court . If the attachment is not returned to the circuit
court on the day of its return , reasonable time should be given to the party to appear
and defend , if he has a defense. Worstell v. Ward, 1 Bush 198 .
§ 7. ( 1. ) Sale by landlord - rents. The assignment of a title bond for land
without any stipulation as to the rents , or when the purchaser shall have possession,
entitles the purchaser to the rents after the date of assignment . Epperson v. Blake-
more , 2 Bush 241 .

372 APPENDIX .

or premises rented, on the fixtures , on the household furniture , and
other personal property of the tenant , or under -tenant, owned by
him , after possession is taken under the lease ; but such lien shall not
be for more than one year's rent due or to become due, nor for any
rent which has been due for more than one hundred and twenty days .
But if any such property be removed openly from the leased prem-
ises , and without fraudulent intent , and not returned, the lien of the
landlord shall be lost as to it, unless the same be asserted by proper
procedure within fifteen days from the day of removal .
§ 14. Liability for wrongfully interfering with property dis-
trained or attached. Any person guilty of a wrongful taking or
removing property distrained or attached for rent , from any person
having the legal custody of it, shall be liable to the person aggrieved
for treble damages , with costs . If the property distrained , after such
wrongful taking or removal, come to the possession of the owner by
his wrongful procurement , he shall be liable to the person aggrieved
in like manner .
§ 15. Upon what terms lien holder may remove property . If,
after the commencement of any tenancy , a lien be created on the
property upon the leased premises liable for rent , the party making
or acquiring such lien may remove the property from the premises
upon the following terms , and not otherwise : that is, by paying to
the person entitled to the rent so much as is in arrear , and securing
to him so much as is to become due ; what is so paid and secured not
being more altogether than a year's rent .
§ 16. Officer levying on property on leased premises to pay
rent-bond of plaintiff to obtain sale. If the property be
which has not been due more than four months before the attachment or distress
warrant is sued out . Fisher v. Kollerts , 16 B. M. 398 ; Williams v. Wood , 2 Met . 41 .
(2.) A landlord does not waive his superior lien for the rent by taking personal
security for the amount of it from the tenant , and the surety of the tenant can pay
the rent and he substituted to all the rights and liens of the landlord upon the
property of the tenant . Smith v. Wells , 4 Bush 92 .
(3.) A landlord's lien is superior to a mortgage lien , created after the property had
been taken to the leased premises , to the extent of one year's rent, if the landlord
enforces his lien within the time allowed ; nor does it matter whether the rent
accrued before or after the creation of the mortgage lien. English v. Duncan , 14
Bush 877 .
(4.) If the property upon which the landlord has a superior lien be openly removed
from the premises , the lien of the landlord upon it is lost if it gets into the hands of
a bona fide purchaser ; although the landlord may pursue it and attempt to assert his
lien before the expiration of fifteen days after the removal . Stone v. Bohm ,
79 Ky. 141 .

interest in or title to rents in arrear whilst she is alive, shall in

§ 22. Remedies of personal representative . The personal rep-
resentatives of a person to whom any rent was due and unpaid at the
time of his death, shall have the same remedy, by action or by dis-
tress , for the recovery of the arrears of such rent that the decedent
would have had if living .
§ 23. Sale of property under distress warrant. If distress. be
made for rent , the officer making the same shall advertise the time
and the place of the sale , in the same manner that sheriffs are directed
to advertise personal property sold under execution , but shall not be
advertised in a newspaper, though a local law may so require in regard
to sales under execution ; and shall sell so much thereof as will satisfy
the rent and all interests and costs , to the highest bidder , on a credit
of three months , with interest from the date , and take from the pur-
chasers bonds with good surety for the sale money , which he shall
return with the warrant , and upon which , at maturity , if the same
be not satisfied , the officer before whom it is returned , or clerk of the
circuit court , shall issue execution directed to any county which the
plaintiff may designate. (Form of bond , page 419..)
§ 24. Rent may be replevied . A distress for rent may , at any
time before a sale of the property , be replevied for three months , by
the defendant's giving bond and good surety , to be approved by the
officer ; whereupon the property seized shall be restored , and the
officer shall return the precept and replevin bond, to be proceeded on
as is provided in case of a sale bond . (Form of bond , page 419. )
§ 25. Indorsement on executions . All executions which shall
issue on a sale or replevin bond , taken on a distress , shall be indorsed
that no security of any kind is to be taken .
§ 26. Damages for wrongfully distraining or attaching . If
property be distrained for any rent not due , or attached for any rent
not due or accruing , or taken under any attachment sued out without
good cause , the owner of such property may , in an action against the
§ 24. Replevin bond. That the amount of rent distrained for was greater than
it should have been will not affect the validity of the replevin bond , nor authorize the
court to set aside the replevin bond and sales made under an execution which issued
thereon . Dean v. Ball , 3 Bush 502 .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - Jurisdiction of Courts and Superior Court
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code details the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals and establishes a Superior Court. It outlines the types of cases and judgments that can be appealed to these courts, including civil actions, claims to office, condemnation of property, road and ferry orders, and will probates. The Superior Court is granted exclusive appellate jurisdiction over final orders and judgments of other Commonwealth courts, with specific exceptions such as the validity of statutes, title to freehold, franchise rights, felony cases, will probates, and monetary judgments over $3,000. It also assumes original jurisdiction in fiscal cases previously held by the Franklin circuit court.

ARTICLE I.
ORIGINAL JURISDICTION .
[ General Statutes , Chapter 28 , Article 4.]
§ 1. The circuit court has original jurisdiction of all matters , both
in law and equity , within its county , of which jurisdiction is not, by
law , exclusively delegated to some other tribunal ; and has all power
necessary to carry into effect the jurisdiction given . ( See as to

out the State .

§ 1. That article four (4) of chapter twenty- eight (28) of the Gen-
eral Statutes be, and the same is hereby , amended as follows : In
actions for the recovery of money or property , when the amount
claimed by the plaintiff does not exceed fifty dollars , exclusive of
interest , and the defendant or defendants are non -residents of Ken-
tucky , and the said defendants are the owners of real estate in this
Commonwealth , but not of personal estate sufficient to satisfy the
demand of the plaintiff , jurisdiction is hereby conferred upon the
circuit and equity and criminal courts of the county where such real
estate is situate to try such action , as though the amount claimed
exceeded fifty dollars ; and the plaintiff shall be entitled to sue out
all such provisional remedies , and to prosecute his demand in the
same way and under the same rules as now apply to similar actions
in said courts , where jurisdiction is now given by law .

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the jurisdiction of the Superior Court and Circuit Courts in civil actions. It details the original jurisdiction of the circuit court over matters not exclusively delegated to other tribunals and grants circuit judges conservatorship of the peace. It also amends previous statutes to confer jurisdiction upon circuit, equity, and criminal courts for actions involving non-resident defendants who own real estate in Kentucky, even if the amount claimed is less than fifty dollars, provided they lack sufficient personal estate. This amendment allows for provisional remedies and prosecution under existing rules for such cases.

ARTICLE 1. Original Jurisdiction .
2. Appellate Jurisdiction .
ARTICLE I.
ORIGINAL JURISDICTION .
[Gen. Stat. , chap . 28 , art . 13.]
§1 . The quarterly court of each county shall have concurrent
original jurisdiction, both in law and equity , with justices of the
peace, in all civil proceedings ; but the jurisdiction shall not attach
except by consent of the defendants in writing , unless one of them
resides in the civil district which embraces the county seat , or all are
non -residents of the county . ( See sec . 710 of Code .)
§ 2. Quarterly courts have jurisdiction concurrent with circuit
courts , of all actions for the recovery of money or personal prop-
erty, where the matter in controversy , exclusive of interest and cost ,
exceeds fifty dollars , and does not exceed one hundred dollars in
value .
Amendment of March 10, 1880 : §1 . That section 2, of article
13 , chapter 28, of the General Statutes, be, and the same is
hereby , so amended as to read as follows : Quarterly courts shall
have jurisdiction concurrent with circuit courts of all actions
for the recovery of money or personal property when the matter
in controversy , exclusive of interest and cost, exceeds fifty dol-
lars and does not exceed two hundred dollars in value .
§ 3. If in any action in the quarterly courts it shall appear, during
the pendency thereof, that the title of real property is involved in the
controversy, it shall be the duty of the court , on the motion of either
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1890, p. 392

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1890 outlines the original and appellate jurisdiction of quarterly courts in Kentucky. It details the types of civil proceedings, including those involving recovery of money or personal property, and the monetary limits for these cases. It also specifies conditions under which the court's jurisdiction attaches, such as consent of defendants or their residency within certain districts. An amendment from March 10, 1880, is included, which adjusts the monetary limit for actions in quarterly courts.

It focuses on the monetary value of claims and the residency of defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

382 APPENDIX .
Jurisdiction of Courts in Civil Actions and Proceedings .
party to the action , to make an order transferring it to the circuit
court of the county , and to deliver the original papers, and certified
copies of all orders made in the action , to the clerk of the circuit
court , who shall docket the same , and on which the same proceed-
ings shall be had as though the action had been brought originally in
that court ; but if judgment be rendered by the quarterly court , the
title to any real estate involved therein shall not be affected thereby .
§ 4. Quarterly courts shall have jurisdiction within their several
counties concurrent with the justices ' courts of motions against con-
stables and their securities, or any one of them, or the personal rep-
resentatives of any one or more of them jointly with the survivors ,
for any money collected by them on an execution , order of sale , or
fee bill , or on any note or account, or other claim put into their hands
for collection , and which , on demand , they shall neglect or refuse to
pay ; and also to issue and try warrants of forcible entry and detainer .
* * * * * * * * * * *
§ 6. The county judge is authorized to grant injunctions and
attachments at law or in equity , in the circuit or other courts of his
county.
[There are a number of acts conferring special jurisdiction on quarterly courts of
various counties , these I have omitted , as the object of this chapter is only to give the
general jurisdiction of courts .-ED .
* *
ARTICLE II .
APPELLATE JURISDICTION .
[Act of May 15 , 1886. Gen. Stat . , page 395. ]
* * * * * * * * *
§ 3. Appeals shall be to the quarterly courts from the orders and
judgments of justices of the peace and police courts in civil cases ,
where the amount in controversy , exclusive of interest and cost, is ten
dollars and not more than fifty .
§ 4. The manner and time of taking appeals from said courts shall
be governed by the provisions of the Civil Code of Practice regulat-
ing appeals from said courts . (Provisions concerning appeals , see
secs . 724 to 732 and notes thereto .)
§ 5. All laws or parts of laws in conflict with this act are hereby
repealed .
§ 6. This act shall take effect from and after its passage ; but the
provisions of this act shall not apply to the city of Louisville .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1890 - County Courts and Justices' Courts Jurisdiction
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the jurisdiction of County Courts and Justices' Courts. County Courts have jurisdiction over granting various licenses (druggist, peddler, retail liquors, tavern), probate of wills, administration of estates, appointment/removal of guardians, establishment of ferries, apprenticing children, settlements with fiduciaries, bastardy cases, road matters, and condemnation of land for mills. Justices' Courts have jurisdiction over civil actions for recovery of money or personal property up to fifty dollars, issuing process, subpœnas, and administering oaths. They also have concurrent jurisdiction with quarterly courts over motions against constables.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the jurisdictional powers of county and justices' courts in Kentucky as of 1890. It enumerates various civil and administrative functions these courts perform, such as granting licenses, handling estates, and managing roads. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural and jurisdictional law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
384 APPENDIX .
Jurisdiction of Courts in Civil Actions and Proceedings .
§ 3. Any person having ajudgment in a justice's court on which
execution has issued and been properly returned , as to the whole or
part thereof, " No property found ," may, before a justice of the peace ,
proceed , by equitable action , to subject to the satisfaction of his judg-
ment any indebtedness to the defendant therein . ( See sec . 439 , Civil
Code.)
§ 4. Any person having a demand arising out of contract not
exceeding fifty dollars , against one or more who are non -residents or
absent from this Commonwealth for a consecutive period of four
months , may , before a justice of the peace , have the remedy of attach-
ment to subject to the payment thereof any choses in action or per-
sonal property of the non -resident or absent debtor .
§ 5. Any creditor who , upon a claim less than fifty dollars , arising
out of contract , commences proceedings thereon before a justice of
the peace , may , upon affidavit that his claim is just , that his demand
will be endangered by delay arising before judgment or return of no
property found upon an execution , and that the defendant has not
property in this Commonwealth subject to execution sufficient to pay
the said demand , have the remedy of attachment to subject to the .
payment thereof any money , choses in action , or equitable interest in
personal property belonging to defendant .
[There are a number of acts conferring special jurisdiction on justices ' courts in
various counties; these I have not thought proper to incorporate .-ED. ]
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1890, p. 395

classification.title: Jurisdiction of Courts in Civil Actions and Proceedings
classification.summary: These sections of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outline procedures for obtaining judgments and attachments in justice's courts. Section 3 allows for equitable actions to satisfy judgments when 'no property found' on execution. Section 4 permits attachment for demands under fifty dollars against non-residents or those absent for four months. Section 5 provides for attachment remedies for claims under fifty dollars if the creditor demonstrates the claim is just, endangered by delay, and the defendant lacks sufficient property in the Commonwealth.

It outlines remedies for creditors against debtors, including provisions for non-residents and absent individuals. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1890.

Petition in Equity .
HENRY CIRCUIT COURT .
The plaintiff , John Smith , states that in April, 1886, he recovered a judgment in
the circuit court for Henry county against the defendant , James Jones , for one
hundred dollars , with six per cent . interest thereon from June 1, 1880 , and ten dollars
costs; that on June 13, 1886 , he had issued from the clerk's office of said court an
execution on said judgment for the amount thereof ; said, execution was directed to
the sheriff of Henry county , made returnable on August 1, 1886, and on June 14,
1886, was placed in the hands of J. W. Ridgway , then sheriff of Henry county , to do
execution thereof . On August 1, 1886 , said sheriff returned said execution to the
office from whence it issued , endorsed , " No property found , J. W. Ridgway , S. H.
C." A copy of said execution and officer's return is filed as part hereof. No part of
zid judgment has been paid .
Wherefore plaintiff prays for a general order of attachment against defendant's
property; that defendant be required to answer and discover any money choses in
action, legal or equitable interests , or other property owned by or in which he has
any interest , and that so much of any property discovered by defendant as may be
necessary be subjected to the payment of plaintiff's judgment and costs of this
action, he prays for all proper relief . J. R. FEARS , Attorney for Plaintiff.
John Smith , Plaintiff,
Z'S.
21. To Enforce Mortgage Lien.
[See secs . 692 , 694.]
James Jones and Mary Jones , Defendants .}
HENRY CIRCUIT COURT .
Petition in Equity .
The plaintiff , John Smith , states that the defendant , James Jones , by his promissory
note, dated June 1, 1886 , and executed and delivered to plaintiff, agreed to pay him
one year thereafter one hundred dollars , with six per cent . interest thereon from date
until paid. No part of said note has been paid. He states that at the time said
note was executed , and to secure its payment , the defendant and his wife defendant ,
Mary Jones, executed to him a mortgage on a tract of land , in Henry county , Ken-
tucky, on the waters of Drennon creek , bounded as follows : Beginning at a stone,
thence east one hundred poles , thence south one hundred poles, thence west one
hundred poles, thence north one hundred poles to the beginning , containing sixty-
three acres . Said mortgage was duly signed and acknowledged by defendants ,
recorded in the proper office , and a copy thereof, together with said note, is filed as
part hereof . Plaintiff states that he has , by virtue of said mortgage , a lien on said
land to secure the payment of said note , that there are no other liens on it known to
him, and it can not be divided without materially impairing its value .
Wherefore plaintiff prays judgment against defendant , James Jones , for his debt ,
interest and costs, that said land be sold and a sufficiency of the proceeds of the sale
to satisfy said judgment be applied to that purpose and for all proper relief .
J. R. FEARS , Attorney for Plaintiff,

[See secs . 65 , and 428 to 438. ]
-John Smith , Administrator of James
Jones , Plaintiff,

and Harve Black , Defendants .
HENRY CIRCUIT COURT .
Petition in Equity .
The plaintiff, John Smith , states that on June -, 1886 , James Jones, then a resi-
dent of Henry county , Kentucky , died intestate , and plaintiff was appointed admin-
istrator of his estate by the Henry county court at its August term , 1886 , and there-
upon qualified , and is now acting as such administrator . Copies of the orders of said
court showing his appointment and qualification are filed as part hereof , marked
" A." He states that said decedent left surviving him as his only heirs at law the
defendants , Mary Jones, his widow , and Wm . Jones , his only child , who is under four-
teen years of age. The decedent owed, at his death , debts amounting toone thousand
dollars , all now due and unpaid , and left no personal estate, except what was set
apart to his widow by the appraisers of his estate. A copy of the inventory and allot-
ment to the widow is filed as part hereof , marked " B. " The defendants , Richard
Thomas and Harve Black, are the only creditors of the decedent known to plaintiff ,
and have claims against his estate amounting to one thousand dollars . Decedent
died seized in fee simple of a tract of land in Henry county , Kentucky , bounded as
follows : Beginning at a stone corner to Davis , thence east one hundred poles , thence
south one hundred poles , thence west one hundred poles , thence north one hundred
poles , containing sixty-three acres . The deed conveying said land to decedent is filed
as part hereof , marked " C. " He owned no other real property known to plaintiff ;
this land is worth about two thousand dollars , and it will be necessary to sell a suffi-
ciency of it to pay the debts of decedent , the funeral expenses and costs of adminis-
tration . The defendant , Mary Jones, is entitled to a homestead in said land .
Wherefore plaintiff prays that this action be referred to the commissioner to ascer-
tain and report the amount of debts against the estate , and to settle plaintiff's
accounts as administrator and make proper allowance to the administrator and his
attorney , and that a sufficiency of said land , after setting apart a homestead , be sold
to pay the debts of decedent and costs . He prays for all proper relief.
J. R. FEARS , Attorney for Plaintiff .

Summons in Circuit Court in Ordinary Action .
[Sec . 40. ]
The Commonwealth of Kentucky to the Sheriff of Henry County :
You are commanded to summon James Jones to answer , on the first day of the
next April term of the Henry circuit court , a petition filed against him in said court
by John Smith , and warn him that, upon his failure to answer , the petition will be
taken for confessed, or he will be proceeded against for contempt ; and you will make
due return of this summons on the first day of the next April term of said court .
Witness , J. S. Douthit, clerk of said court , this March 1, 1888 .
S. J. DOUTHіт , С. Н. С. С.
Summons in Circuit Court in Equitable Action.
[The summons in equitable actions is the same as in ordinary actions , except that
the words " in equity " should be inserted after the word " petition ."]

[Sec . 701. ]

You are commanded to summon James Jones to answer in my court , at its next
October term, or in the court of any justice of the peace in Henry county to which

classification.title: Forms in Civil Actions - Affidavits
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines various forms of affidavits used in civil actions. These include affidavits to have a guardian ad litem appointed for a minor defendant, affidavits to request a warning order for a non-resident or absent defendant, and affidavits to require a party to personally verify their pleading. The examples provided use generic names and do not contain any racial indicators or discriminatory provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural legal forms (affidavits) for civil actions in Kentucky courts. It covers situations like appointing a guardian for a minor, serving absent or non-resident defendants, and verifying pleadings. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are standard legal practices applicable to all litigants regardless of race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Forms in Civil Actions - Attachment
classification.summary: This document contains forms related to civil actions, specifically for attachments. It includes an 'Appraisement Before Taking Bond' form, an 'Affidavit for General Attachment', a 'Bond of Plaintiff to Obtain Attachment', and an 'Order of General Attachment'. These forms detail the process of attaching a defendant's property in a civil lawsuit, including the valuation of property, the plaintiff's sworn statement regarding the claim and the defendant's intent to defraud creditors, and the bond required to secure the attachment.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details legal procedures for civil attachments, including forms for appraisement, affidavits, bonds, and orders. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute pertains to general legal processes applicable to all citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The affiant , John Smith , states that on March 1 , 1887, he rented his farm , in
Henry county , Kentucky , to James Jones , for the year ending March 1, 1888 ; that
said Jones agreed to pay him on March 1, 1888 , as rent for said farm , one hundred
dollars; that no part of said rent has been paid, and that there are reasonable grounds
for belief, and that he believes , unless an attachment be issued he will lose his said

Notice.

HENRY CIRCUIT COURT .
The defendant , James Jones, is notified that the plaintiff, John Smith , will, on
May 10, 1888 , at the law office of William Giles , in the town of New Castle , Henry
county, Kentucky , take the depositions of sundry witnesses to be read as evidence in
his behalf on the trial of the above styled case, and will continue the taking from
day to day until through . WILLIAM GILES , Attorney for Plaintiff.

notice must give names of witnesses . Sec. 566. ]
Subpœna-Warrant of Arrest .
[Sec . 578. ]

officer taking depositions , may be similar to forms under " Subpœna -Warrant of
Arrest ," on page 421 .
Caption of Deposition ,
[Sec . 582. ]
The deposition of Ben Allen , taken on May 10, 1888, at the law office of William
Giles , in the town of New Castle , to be read as evidence in an action between John

Certificate of Examining Officer.
State of Kentucky , Henry County :

son , or by agent or attorney , and , if any , who was present .]
Given under my hand , this May 10, 1888 . JOSEPH THOMASSON, Examiner .

land as may be necessary to satisfy said debt, interest and costs be sold for that pur-
pose . C. T. Scott , Master Commissioner of the Henry circuit court , will make said
sale at the courthouse door , in New Castle , Kentucky , on the first day of some regu-
lar term of the circuit or county court for said county , at public outcry , to the highest
and best bidder , on a credit of six months . He will take from the purchaser bond
with good surety for the purchase price, payable to the plaintiff, and bearing six per

AN ACT
REGULATING
PRACTICE IN CRIMINAL CASES .
PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS .
1. Name of the act . Regulates all prosecutions and penal actions .
2. Proceedings commenced before January 1, 1877 , how to be prosecuted .
3. All laws within the purview of the Code repealed .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§1 . That the provisions of this act shall regulate the proceedings
in all prosecutions and penal actions in all the courts of this Com-
monwealth , from and after the first day of January , 1877 , and shall
be known as " The Code of Practice in Criminal Cases ."
§ 2. Be it further enacted , That all prosecutions or proceedings in
criminal or penal cases , which shall be commenced before the first
day of January , 1877 , and which, by the existing laws , would be
valid , shall not be rendered invalid by this act, but may be prose-
cuted to their conclusion , and enforced according to the existing
laws , as if this act had not been passed .
§ 3. Be it further enacted , That all laws coming within the purview
of this act shall become repealed when this act goes into effect , except
as provided in the preceding section.

classification.summary: This act, effective January 1, 1877, regulates all criminal prosecutions and penal actions in Kentucky courts. It repeals all prior laws within its purview, except for proceedings commenced before its effective date. The act also clarifies that certain provisions regarding search warrants for gaming tables remain in effect, and that a prosecutor is no longer necessary for an indictment for assault and battery.

§ 11 [8] . What offenses may be prosecuted by penal action .
A public offense , of which the only punishment is a fine , may be
prosecuted by a penal action in the name of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , or in the name of an individual or corporation , if the whole
fine be given to such individual or corporation . The proceedings in
penal actions are regulated by the Code of Practice in civil actions .

behavior .
3. For arresting and confining insane, drunken , and disorderly
persons . (Proceedings to suppress riots , sec . 371 ; to keep the peace ,
sec . 382 ; to arrest drunken persons , sec . 394.)

§ 14 [11 ] . Indictment in circuit court gives jurisdiction of all
degrees of offense charged . If an indictment be found in the cir-
cuit court for an offense within its jurisdiction, such court shall have
jurisdiction in that prosecution of all the degrees of such offense , and
of all offenses included in the one charged , although some of those
degrees or included offenses are within the exclusive jurisdiction of
an inferior or local court . ( What offenses included in one charged ;
degrees of offenses defined , secs . 262 to 266. )
§ 15 [12 ] . When and how prosecutions removed from circuit
to inferior court . When prosecutions have been commenced in the
circuit court , of which a city, magistrates ' or police court has con-
current jurisdiction, they may, by an order of the circuit court , be
removed into such city, magistrates ' , or police court , for trial. Upon
such order being made , the clerk of the circuit court shall deliver to
the clerk of the city, magistrates ' or police court , all the papers relat-
ing to the prosecutions , and a copy of the order of removal , and there-
upon the defendant shall be bound to appear in said police , magistrates '
or city court , and the prosecution be carried on to final judgment and
execution , as if commenced in such court ..
§ 16 [ 13 ] . Circuit judges may require grand juries to state
where misdemeanor committed . The judges of circuit courts may
require the grand juries, in prosecutions for misdemeanors within the
local jurisdiction of city or police courts , to state in the indictments
that such misdemeanors were committed within the town or city , or
other local limits of the jurisdiction of the city or police courts .
§ 17 [14] . Territorial jurisdiction of Senate and Court of
Appeals . The jurisdiction of the Senate and Court of Appeals em-
braces the whole Commonwealth .
§ 18 [15 ] . Local jurisdiction of circuit and justices courts .
The local jurisdiction of circuit courts and justices ' courts shall be of
§ 14. The city court of Henderson had , by act of 1867, exclusive jurisdiction of

general law controls local statutes unless there is manifested in the latter a special
intent to repeal the general law . Com . v. Pointer , 5 Bush 301.

§ 19 [16] . Local jurisdiction of police or city courts . The
local jurisdiction of police or city courts shall be of offense , com-
mitted within the limits of the jurisdiction of such courts , as pre-
scribed by the special statutes creating or regulating them . ( What
are police courts , sec . 321. See further as to such courts sec . 306.)
§ 20 [17] . Jurisdiction of offenses committed on river be-
tween two counties . If a river be the boundary between two coun-
ties, the criminal jurisdiction of each county shall embrace offenses
committed on the river, or on any island thereof .
§ 21 [18 ] . When offense may be prosecuted in either of sev-
eral counties . If an offense be committed partly in one and partly
in another county , or if acts and their effects constituting an offense
occur in different counties, the jurisdiction is in either county .
Amendment of May 18 , 1886 : §1 . That section 21 of the
Code of Practice in criminal cases be amended by adding to said
section the following : " But all prosecutions against persons
publishing a newspaper for any libelous matter contained therein
shall be had in the county where the same is printed and issued,

§ 22 [ 19 ] . In what county person guilty of importing prop-

§ 20. Over Ohio river. The boundary and jurisdiction of this State extends to
low water mark on the western or north -western side of the Ohio river, and one who
commits an offense on that river may be punished by the courts of this State . Мс-
Fall v. Com. , 2 Met. 394. See Gen. Stat ., chap . 29 , art . 2, sec . 2.
§ 21. ( 1.) Jurisdiction-larceny . Receiving stolen property, knowing it to be
stolen , is a complete offense, and the circuit court of the county in which the property
is received, and not of the county in which the larceny is committed , has jurisdiction
of the offense . Allison v. Com ., 83 Ky . 254 .
(2. ) A thief can be indicted for a complete larceny either in the county where he
first took the property , or in any county into which , the intent to steal continuing , he
carries it. Ib.
(3. ) Accessory . When the principal crime is committed in one county and the
accessorial acts are done in another , the accessory must be indicted in the county in
which the accessorial acts are done . Tully v. Com ., 13 Bush 142 .
(4.) False pretense. Where false pretenses are made in one State and the money
or property obtained in another , the indictment should be found in county of latter,
where money or property was obtained . Com . v. Van Tuyl , 1 Met. 1.
§ 22. Importing property . Where a horse was stolen in Tennessee and
brought into this State and sold , the thief could be prosecuted in the county in which
he disposed of the property . Ferrill v. Com ., 1 Duv . 153 .

exceeding their criminal jurisdiction . (How writ obtained and pros-
ecuted , see Civil Code sec . 474.)
Act of March 18 , 1876 , to confer on quarterly court jurisdiction
of misdemeanors in certain cases : §1 . When any person shall
be lodged in jail in default of bail, charged with a misdemeanor,
or whether committed to jail under an indictment or by the order
of an examining court , it shall be the duty of the jailer to notify
the judge of the quarterly court and county attorney of the fact ,
if the court in which the prisoner is indicted , or is ordered to
appear, is not in session .
§ 2. The judge of the quarterly court shall thereupon call
upon the clerk of the court in which the prisoner stands indicted ,
or is ordered to appear , and said clerk shall make out and deliver
to said judge a certified copy of the record in his possession , by
virtue of which the prisoner was arrested and is detained .
§ 3. Said judge shall cause the prisoner to be brought before
him at the courthouse ; shall inform him of the charge against
him ; fix a time on that or another day for the trial ; issue sum-
mons for witnesses , and have a jury summoned. If the prisoner
has no attorney , and is too poor to employ one, the court shall ,
at his request , appoint an attorney to defend him .
§ 4. The trial shall be conducted in all respects as if it were
in the circuit court . The judge shall , as clerk, do whatever the
clerk of a circuit court should do in such trial,keeping in a book

It details procedures for appeals from quarterly court judgments to circuit court, the role of the county attorney in prosecuting misdemeanors, and the transfer of indictments from circuit courts to quarterly courts. It also specifies that the provisions do not apply to Jefferson County.

classification.title: Persons arrested for misdemeanor may give bail at once-sheriff may take.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for individuals arrested for misdemeanors to post bail. It specifies that bail can be given before the issuing magistrate or county court judge for examination, or before the trial court. The warrant of arrest must be endorsed with the bail amount and authorization for the sheriff to accept it. The officer taking bail must set a court date within five days, with extensions possible for arrests made in different counties.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes procedural aspects of criminal justice, specifically concerning bail for misdemeanor arrests. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would inherently or implicitly discriminate based on race. The procedures described are standard legal processes applicable to all individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Arrest-by Whom and How Made .
guilty , or from his own personal knowledge , shall be deemed
guilty of a misdemeanor , and , on conviction thereof by indictment
and trial in the circuit court , fined in any sum not less than one
hundred nor more than two hundred dollars .
§ 32 [29 ] . Magistrate may examine witnesses to ascertain
offender . A magistrate , if satisfied that a felony has been com-
mitted , shall have power to summon before him any person he may
think proper for examination on oath concerning it, to enable him to
ascertain the offender and to issue a warrant for his arrest .
Amendment of March 7 , 1884 : §1 . That section 32, title 3,
chapter 1 , of the Criminal Code of Practice , title " Proceedings
for the Arrest of Criminals ," be amended so as to read as fol-
lows , viz : A magistrate , if satisfied that any public offense has
been committed , shall have power to summon before him any
person he may think proper for examination on oath concerning
it, to enable him to ascertain the offender, and to issue a warrant
for his arrest .
§ 33 [ 30 ] . What officers return on warrant must state . The
officer who has executed a warrant of arrest shall make a written
return on the warrant , of the time and manner of executing it, and
deliver the warrant to the magistrate before whom the defendant is
brought ; or, if bail be given , as provided in section 28 , the officer
shall deliver the warrant and bail -bond to the magistrate before whom ,
or to the clerk of the court in which , the defendant is bound by the
bail -bond to appear .
§ 34 [ 31 ] . When officer may transmit warrant by mail . If
the arrest be made in a different county from that in which the
offense is charged to have been committed , and bail be there given ,
the officer may transmit the warrant and bail-bond by mail to the
person to whom , by the last section, he is required to deliver them .

Arrest , by Whom and How Made .

§ 35 [32] . Who may arrest . An arrest may be made by a peace
officer, or by a private person .
§ 36 [ 33 ] . When peace officer may arrest. A peace officer may
make an arrest-
1. In obedience to a warrant of arrest delivered to him .
2. Without a warrant , when a public offense is committed in
his presence , or when he has reasonable grounds for believing
that the person arrested has committed a felony . (See further as
to arrest by peace officer without warrant , secs . 383 , 394, 395.)
§ 37 [ 34 ] . When private person may arrest . A private per-
son may make an arrest , when he has reasonable grounds for believ-
ing that the person arrested has committed a felony .
§ 38 [ 35 ] . When magistrate or judge may orally order arrest .
A magistrate , or any judge , may orally order a peace officer or pri-
vate person to arrest any one committing a public offense in the
magistrate's or judge's presence , which order shall authorize the
arrest . ( See further as to oral direction to arrest , sec . 383.)
§ 39 [ 36 ] . Person must be informed of intention to arrest him
and cause of arrest . The person making the arrest shall inform
the person about to be arrested of the intention to arrest him , and of
the offense charged against him for which he is to be arrested , and ,
if acting under a warrant of arrest, shall give information thereof,
and, if required , shall show the warrant .
§ 40 [ 37 ] . When officer may break doors to arrest . To make
an arrest, an officer may break open the door of a house in which the
§ 36. ( 1. ) Arrest. " There is no provision of the Code authorizing any other
person than a peace officer to make an arrest in obedience to a warrant of arrest. "
The sheriff can not authorize a private person to execute a warrant of arrest . Salis-
bury v . Com . , 79 Ky . 425 ; as to the law concerning the murder of an officer while
attempting to make an arrest , see Mockabee v. Com ., 78 Ky . , 380 ; Fleetwood v.
Com. , 80 Ky. 1 ; Creighton v. Com . , 84 Ky . 103 ; Creighton v. Com ., 83 Ky . 142 .
(2.) A peace officer can only make an arrest without a warrant " when a public
offense is committed in his presence , or when he has reasonable grounds for believing
that the person arrested has committed a felony ," and this provision applies to
policemen in the city of Louisville . Jamison v. Gaernett , 10 Bush 221 .

It also covers the use of force and breaking doors to effect an arrest.

Arrest -by Whom and How Made .

§ 41 [ 38 ] . Officer may summon aid-penalty for failing to
assist . An officer making an arrest may orally summon as many
persons as he deems necessary to aid in making the arrest, and all
persons failing , without reasonable excuse , to obey the summons ,
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor , and be punished by fine and impris-
onment , or either .
§ 42 [ 39 ] . How arrest is made . An arrest is made by placing
the person of the defendant in restraint , or by his submitting to the
custody of the person making the arrest .
§ 43 [40 ] . No unnecessary force to be used. No unnecessary
force or violence shall be used in making the arrest .
§ 44 [41 ] . Prisoner escaping may be pursued and recaptured .
If the defendant , after an arrest , escape or be rescued , the person in
whose custody he was may immediately pursue and recapture him in
any part of the Commonwealth .
§ 45 [42 ] . How officer arresting under warrant to proceed .
The officer making the arrest in obedience to a warrant shall proceed
with the defendant as directed by the warrant .
§ 46 [43 ] . Proceedings when arrest made without warrant .
If an arrest be made without a warrant , whether by a peace officer
or private person , the defendant shall be forthwith carried before the
most convenient magistrate of the county in which the arrest is made ,
and the grounds on which the arrest was made shall be stated to the
magistrate ; and if the offense for which the arrest was made be
charged to have been committed in a different county from that in
which the arrest was made , and the magistrate believe, from the
statements made to him on oath, that there are sufficient grounds for
an examination , he shall , by his written order , commit the defendant
to a peace officer, to be conveyed by him before a magistrate of the
county in which the offense is charged to have been committed ; or ,
if the offense be a misdemeanor, the defendant may give bail before
the magistrate for his appearance before the judge of the county
court of the county in which the offense was committed , on a day to
§ 46. Bail . A magistrate has no authority to take a bail bond from a prisoner
brought before him for examination of a charge of felony committed in another
county , nor has he any right to take temporary bail for the appearance of the accused
before him . Com . v. Salyer , 8 Bush 461 .

TITLE IV .] 19PROCEEDINGS IN THE EXAMINING COURT .
Proceedings in the Examining Court .
§ 49 [ 46 ] . Magistrate may examine charge and commit or
hold to bail . A magistrate of the county in which a public offense
has been committed is authorized to examine the charge, and com-
mit to jail or hold to bail the person charged with its commission ,
except as provided in section 71. ( See act at end of sec . 71. Bail may
be sworn ; qualifications of, secs . 76 , 77.)
§ 50 [47 ] . Proceedings when defendant first brought before
magistrate . When a person who has been arrested shall be brought ,
or, in pursuance of a bail bond, shall come, before a magistrate of
the county in which the offense is charged to have been committed ,
the charge shall be forthwith examined as directed in this title .
§ 51 [47] . Duty of magistrates before commencing examina-
tion-notice to county attorney in felonies . The magistrate ,
before commencing the examination , shall state the charge, and
inquire of the defendant whether he desires the aid of counsel , and
shall allow a reasonable opportunity for procuring it. He shall also ,
if the offense charged be a felony , give to the county attorney , if
not present , reasonable notice in writing , of not more than one day ,
to attend and prosecute such charge . The notice may be served by
any sheriff , constable or marshal , and it shall be the duty of such
officer to serve the same when placed in his hands . (Felony defined ,
sec . 6.)
§ 52. Defendant may object to the magistrate . If a defendant
shall make and present his affidavit that he does not believe that he
can secure a fair trial , and examination , before the judge , officer, or
justices constituting the court , he or they shall retire from the case ,
and another or other magistrates shall take his or their places ; but
where only one of the two justices is objected to, he only shall retire
from the case , and another justice shall take his place. When a
judge or justice has retired from the case, by reason of the filing of
the affidavit as provided above , and his place has been supplied by
another judge or justice , no affidavit can be filed nor objection made
by the party first objecting , to parties then constituting the court .
§ 49. ( 1. ) Magistrates -power of. Judges of county , or police , or city courts ,
mayors , chairman of the trustees of towns and justices of the peace are magistrates
(sec. 26 ) and any one of them may commit or hold to bail except as provided in sec .
71 ; see amendment to that section . Com . v. Cummings , 18 B. M. 26 ; Rice v. Com ., 8
Bush 14 .
(2.) Sunday-bail . A bail bond or recognizance executed on Sunday before an
officer authorized to take the same is legal and binding on the defendant and his
sureties . Rice v. Com ., 3 Bush 14 ; Watts v. Com ., 5 Bush 309 .
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1890, p. 568

classification.title: Proceedings in the Examining Court
classification.summary: This section of the 1890 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures for examining charges in a magistrate's court. It details the magistrate's authority to examine charges, commit or hold individuals to bail, and the proceedings when a defendant is brought before the court. It also specifies the magistrate's duty to inform the defendant of their rights, including the aid of counsel, and to notify the county attorney in felony cases. Additionally, it provides a mechanism for the defendant to object to a magistrate if they believe a fair trial cannot be secured.

It outlines the powers of magistrates, the process of bringing a defendant before the court, and the rights of the defendant during this preliminary stage. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language used is procedural and applies generally to all individuals brought before the court. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

20 [TITLE IV.PROCEEDINGS IN THE EXAMINING COURT .
Proceedings in the Examining Court .
§ 53. Proceedings when objection to magistrate is made.
When the affidavit provided for in section 52 is filed , and there is but
one magistrate ; or if there be two and the objection apply to both ; if
the defendant be in custody, the peace officer, having him in custody ,
shall take him before some other magistrate to be examined . If the
defendant be on bail , the examination shall be continued , and the
peace officer in attendance shall notify another magistrate to attend
and preside in the case . If the examination be before two justices
of the peace , and the objection apply to but one of them, the other
shall associate another justice with himself and proceed as directed in
section 71 .
§ 54 [48 ] . Examination may be adjourned not exceeding two
days . For the purpose of procuring the attendance of witnesses ,
or for other sufficient reasons , the magistrate may adjourn the exam-
ination from time to time, not , however , exceeding two days at a
time.
§ 55 [ 49 ] . Disposition of defendant during adjournment .
During the periods of adjournment the defendant shall be committed
to jail , or to the custody of a peace officer ; but if the offense be a
misdemeanor , the defendant may give bail , or the magistrate may fix
a sum equivalent to bil , upon which being deposited with the peace
officer in attendance , unless he be a policeman , as security for the
defendant's appearance at the time or times appointed , he shall be
released from custody during the periods of the adjournment of the
examination .
Amendment of February 6, 1882 : §1 . That section 55 of the
Criminal Code of Practice be amended to read as follows , viz :
" During the periods of adjournment the defendant may give
bail when the offense with which he is charged is a bailable one .
The court may , in bailable cases , fix a sum equivalent to bail ,
and if the defendant, or any one for him, deposit money to the
amount named with the magistrate in attendance , unless he be a
policeman , as security for the defendant's appearance at the time
or times appointed , he shall be released from custody during the
periods of the adjournment of the examination ; but if the
defendant fail to give bail , or to deposit money as indicated here-
§ 55. Bail during adjournment . It was held that a bail bond taken during
the progress of the examination , where the defendant was charged with a felony , was
unauthorized and void . Com . v. Moore , 3 Met . 477. But see now amendment to this
section .
